hints.text: Most common plate: **all-yellow** (Gen4 also shows one w/ a big orange circle); **front plates required**
hints.uniq_note: The all-yellow plate reads clearly through the blur — a fast Alaska tell within the US.

hints.text: Alaska highway shield features the **Big Dipper constellation** bottom-left
Trees **thinner & less broad-leaved** than in the other 49 states; tall **snow-covered mountains** visible on a lot of coverage

Camera mix unlike the lower 48: fair amount of **Gen2**; Gen4 **smallcam** (white car front visible); and **shitcam** (low-res, faded colours, big bottom blur) — the only US state where shitcam is prevalent
hints.uniq_note: US-looking Gen2 or shitcam round → think Alaska first.

hints.text: **Gen4 concentrated around Anchorage**; interior & north = subarctic/tundra (short trees, little undergrowth); **aspens** in lower interior/south-central; farmland only in the south (Matanuska Valley, Kenai Peninsula)

**Alexander Archipelago** (panhandle, BC border): tall lush green mountains, wooden buildings, misty overcast Gen2 (coastal OR/WA can look similar); **Juneau** = the panhandle's only built-up city, ringed by forested mountains
The **Trans-Alaska pipeline** is often visible from main roads — nothing similar exists in the rest of the US (map of viewpoints)

Only two major urban areas: **Anchorage & Fairbanks**; Anchorage suburbs = wide roads + commercial strips + mountains behind (Fairbanks similar but fewer mountains); fire hydrants: **pale pink/pale yellow → Anchorage**, bright yellow or yellow-blue → Fairbanks; shitcam only around Anchorage/Fairbanks + their connecting road

**Highway spotlights**: Dalton Hwy north end = flat, snowy, overcast (hill ridge west); Denali Hwy (Cantwell↔Paxson) = E-W gravel Gen2, late summer wildflowers, big mountains; Steese Hwy (Fairbanks↔Central) = similar but noticeably lower mountains; Hwy 5 (Yukon border↔Tetlin Jct) = foggy rainy Gen2 — US side has outer lines & is unpaved east of Chicken (Canadian Top-of-the-World: paved, no outer lines)

**Prudhoe Bay** = northernmost US town w/ coverage: dead cold landscape, overcast, sometimes a 2nd Google car; **Nome, Unalakleet, Galena** = trekker-only towns (lime-green hat or B&W beanie; the B&W bandana appears only in Nome)
Plates: blue strips both sides OR a **single red strip on the left**
hints.uniq_note: The red-left-strip plate is unique in Europe. The blue-strip variant however looks Italian — Italian FRONT plates are distinctly shorter.

shared_with.note: Italian plates also have blue strips on both sides.
shared_with.diff: Italian front plates are distinctly shorter; red strip on the left → always Albania.

hints.text: All Gen4 = **smallcam**: camera mounted low, big circular blur w/ small front protrusion (blur can be fully transparent)

shared_with.note: Most of the Balkans and southern Europe have smallcam.
shared_with.diff: Smallcam only narrows to the region — use plates/language to decide.
Language + bollard bottom: white-painted → Albania/Italy identical, check plates.

hints.text: **Rifts** (sky tears from poor stitching) rarely appear in Albanian Gen3

shared_with.note: Rifts are equally common in Montenegro; rarely in various other countries.
shared_with.diff: Horizontal-bar poles → Albania; ме/Cyrillic bits → Montenegro.

hints.text: Bollards: **black top, red + grey reflector, white-painted bottom**

shared_with.note: Italy uses the same kind of bollards.
shared_with.diff: Smallcam + Mercedes + red soil → Albania; EU plates → Italy.

hints.text: Poletops w/ an **upside-down triangle** shape; simpler poles often have **horizontal bars**

shared_with.note: If unsure between Montenegro and Albania and you see horizontal-bar poles → guess Albania.

hints.text: Chevrons **white on black**

shared_with.note: Same white-on-black chevron in southern Europe.
Montenegro uses two DIFFERENT chevrons: black-on-white and red-on-yellow.

hints.text: Backs of road signs often **painted black**

shared_with.note: Black sign backs common there too.
Black sign backs common there too.

hints.text: Stop-sign **"S" has a distinct shape**: short ends angled like the middle (not vertical) — sometimes on directional signs too

shared_with.note: Italian stop + directional signs have the same S.

hints.text: Speed-sign numbers in a **bold font**

shared_with.note: Montenegrin speed-sign font is much thinner.

hints.text: **Flat, wide guardrail endings** that bend away from the road

shared_with.note: The same endings are found in Italy.

hints.text: **Albanian**: not closely related to any European language — distinct look w/ **Ë ë** (& sometimes Ç ç); street = **rruga**
Mountainous, Mediterranean climate; green mountains w/ rock patches; flat agricultural valleys in between; lots of **exposed red soil** next to roads

shared_with.note: Greece also has similar red soil, especially near the Albanian border; in Montenegro it is less common.

hints.text: Lots of **cylinder water tanks on roofs**; Italian-style buildings (pastel, orange tiled roofs, blocky w/ balconies)

shared_with.note: Water tanks are less common in Montenegro.
Parts of Turkey have similar rooftop water tanks.

hints.text: **Mercedes** is by far the most popular car brand — esp. the classic sedan style
hints.uniq_note: As a *dominant* share of traffic this is an Albania tell within Europe; single Mercedes obviously prove nothing.

hints.text: Gen3 car+camera meta splits the country in two: **long antenna + rifts in the sky** vs **short antenna + no rifts** (community map exists)
Elevation: lowlands in the **west** (Shkodër, Tirana, Vlorë — farmland + low hills) + the flat **Korçë plateau** SE; highest, almost alpine mountains far **north & far south**
**Spotlights**:
bullets: Theth NP road: tall jagged mountains w/ bare mountainsides
Eastern Montenegrin border: valley w/ tall fully-exposed cliff sides
Maja e Papingut (south): massive mountain w/ near-vertical cliff top
SH71 Pishaj–Kokel: wide, almost dried-out river valley SE→NW
SH4 Vlorë–Orikum: Bay of Vlorë w/ Karaburun peninsula + Sazan island
NW of Vlorë: coastal Aleppo-pine forest on small roads
SH4 Levan–Tepelenë + Greek border: double outer lines w/ wide gap

hints.text: **Divided highways** (only a handful covered):
bullets: A1 Fushë Milot–Thumanë: the only one w/ **triple guardrail**
SH2 Durrës–Tirana & SH4 Lushnjë–Rrogozhinë: simple concrete blocks
Tirana/Durrës + A3/SH3 south of Tirana: concrete blocks w/ grass in between
SH4 Durrës–Fier: basic double guardrail, no mountains
E851: the only double-guardrail road going straight through mountains
A2 north of Vlorë: flat surroundings + extra-tall fence on the divider

hints.text: **Lezhë**: old castle on a tall hill; **Gjirokastër**: city on the western hillside of a wide valley
US territory east of Fiji (counts as US); green tropical mountains + palms, small **concrete roads**, one-storey boxy houses w/ plain **metal-sheet roofs**
Identify the island by the truck: **Tutuila** (main, most coverage) = a rusted **black truck bed**
**Manu'a & Olosega** = silver-grey truck front w/ side mirrors; **Tau** (easternmost) = glossy black truck
Single-lane **bridge** joining Manu'a & Olosega w/ a thin wire guardrail; Ofu Airport = concrete runway, car w/ two orange roof straps
Plates w/ an **orange dot on the left** — within Europe unique to Andorra
Only **Gen2 + Gen3** coverage; all Gen3 = medium-short antenna, shot in **October → autumn colours** everywhere; Gen2 mostly overwritten (few small off-roads)
hints.uniq_note: No Gen4 at all + country-wide autumn = a strong Andorra combo in a Pyrenees-looking round.

hints.text: Lots of **stone buildings** — almost everywhere in the country

shared_with.note: Same style just across the border in the French Pyrenees.
Same style in the Spanish Pyrenees.

hints.text: **Wooden snow poles** in a variety of colours
**Catalan** is the main language: Romance, close to Spanish/French — **'i' instead of 'y' for 'and'**, more words ending in consonants; street = **carrer**

shared_with.note: Catalan is also used in Catalonia, Valencia and the Balearics.
shared_with.diff: Catalan + orange-dot plates + mountains everywhere → Andorra.

hints.text: km markers w/ **CG / CS road numbers**
hints.uniq_note: CG/CS numbering is Andorra-only; the marker DESIGN itself resembles Spain's.

hints.text: Guardrails w/ **yellow reflectors**

shared_with.note: Yellow guardrail reflectors also in Spain.
Belgium is the only other European country with yellow reflectors.

hints.text: Bollards w/ an **orange reflector** (occasionally on main highways)

shared_with.note: This bollard can also be found in Spain.

hints.text: **Yellow & turquoise-green traffic cones** used country-wide
Pedestrian signs have **8 stripes**

shared_with.note: Spain is the only other European country with 8-stripe ped signs.

hints.text: Lots of **Gen2 snow trekkers**, especially on ski resorts — the most in Europe for its size
hints.uniq_note: Other countries have snow trekkers too, but ski-resort Gen2 trekker rounds lean heavily Andorra.

hints.text: **Andorra la Vella**: capital surrounded by high mountains — highest European capital (1023 m); look for the **Caldea tower + sky wheel**
**Pas de la Casa** (east, 2080 m): touristy shop/hotel town w/ **treeless mountains** all around
Mostly **snow and ice**; any soil is **always dark**
**Penguins** appear in parts of the coverage — handy to rule out the Arctic
**Deception Island** = blue-and-white ship in the bay, abandoned equipment, a black-sand beach w/ penguins; **Half Moon Island** = tripod w/ people in red jackets
**McMurdo Station** = largest base — dark brown soil, many buildings, American infrastructure, sunset coverage (sun setting in the **south**, so don't guess the N hemisphere)
The **South Pole** tripod = a ring of national flags + a 'Geographic South Pole' sign (often placed on community world maps)
Two plate types: **black w/ a black dot in the middle** (visible through blur) or white w/ **blue top bar**
hints.uniq_note: The black dot is Argentina-only; the blue bar also exists in Brazil.

shared_with.note: White plates w/ blue top bar exist there too.

hints.text: Gen3 = **black Google car**; Gen4 **smallcam** exists

shared_with.note: Peru also has a black Gen3 car (and smallcam).
shared_with.diff: Chevrons: white-red → AR; landscape decides too.

shared_with.note: Uruguay also has a black Gen3 car.
shared_with.diff: Square poles → Uruguay; A-frame wood → Argentina.

hints.text: Very common: **round concrete poles w/ 3 alternating lines**; **doubled-up poles**; wooden poles w/ or w/o crossbar — **wooden A-frame poles = Argentina-only in S. America**
hints.uniq_note: A-frames seal it; doubled concrete poles also occur in Bolivia.

hints.text: Chevrons **white-and-red** — the only country in the Americas with these
Road lines: mostly **dashed white, double yellow, or a mix**
km markers white w/ **black top strip + road number**: **RN** = national (white label on the map), **RP** = provincial (green label)
**Yellow & red rectangular reflectors** on guardrails

shared_with.note: Same reflectors in Uruguay — but the guardrail SUPPORT shape differs.

hints.text: Default vibe: relatively **flat, treeless, sparsely populated** — wide-open desolate feel
Several cities have **unique recognisable taxis** (map)
**Landscape by region**:
bullets: Far west = mountainous (Andes); east rarely has mountains
Misiones: red soil (Brazil-like — check other clues!)
Chaco/Formosa: sandy soil + low trees & bushes
Pampas core: grassland w/ scattered tree plantations; small lakes → Buenos Aires province
San Luis: reddish pavement lined w/ needle-grass
Central-south: flat & barren
Tierra del Fuego / SE-Santa Cruz: flat grass, almost no bushes; beech forests → TdF
Tree plantations → Entre Ríos (also UY/S-Brazil)
Poplar rows + flatness → around Neuquén

hints.text: **Flora**:
bullets: Caranday WAX palm (low fan forests): Formosa (+ Chaco, Corrientes, N-Santa Fe)
Caranday palm (compact fans, dead-leaf coat): Sierras de Córdoba, San Luis
Butia yatay palm (arching, often leaning): Corrientes + El Palmar NP
Totai palm (thin leaves, spear top): wild in Formosa/Chaco/Misiones
Cardón cactus (candelabra, golden spines): high NW (Catamarca, Tucumán, Salta, Jujuy)
Toothpick cactus (tree-like): Catamarca↔Córdoba + Chaco/Salta/Formosa border
Wild sunflowers: Salta, Jujuy, Tucumán
Caldén tree (sinuous branches): La Pampa
Pampas grass (white fluffy heads): Pampas region

hints.text: **Crops**: sugarcane → Tucumán (+ Salta/Jujuy); sorghum (dark-red heads) → Santa Fe/Córdoba/Buenos Aires; corn → Córdoba + Santiago del Estero; sunflower fields → S-Chaco (+ BA/S-Córdoba)

**Province infrastructure**: Latvian-style wooden **hook poles** → only La Rioja & Mendoza; single side insulator → Mendoza; white vertical pole stickers → Tucumán; unique provincial road markers → Córdoba; yellow T-junction signs w/ chevron pattern + road numbers → La Pampa

**Spotlights (north/west)**: Bolivia-like red bricks → W-Jujuy; salt pans → Jujuy (also SW-Bolivia/NE-Chile); red mountains → RN68 valley, S-Salta; Gen4 volcanic rocks → RP43 south of Antofagasta de la Sierra; RN65 (W-Tucumán) = bendy unpaved mountain road into the clouds; Talampaya NP (RN76, W-La Rioja) = red soil + barren ranges; lower mountains → west of Córdoba; **horse trekker** → SW-San Juan (similar one in Chile!); Mendoza outskirts = vineyards w/ Andes west

**Spotlights (south/east)**: Mar del Plata area = quartzite facades + orange tiles + vaulted porches; semi-coastal grass hills → NE of Bahía Blanca; monkey-puzzle trees → W-Neuquén (road 23; also Chile); **'sawdust' car** (sandy dust on black back) → centre-south; rock formations → Villa Dique Florentino Ameghino (NE-Chubut); Comodoro Rivadavia on Chenque hill slopes; snow coverage → eastern RP25 (E-Santa Cruz); melting snow → RN293 (SW-Santa Cruz); snowy mountains to the north → **Ushuaia** (southernmost city)

videos.title: Never Mix Up Australia and South Africa Again
How To Always Guess the Correct State in Australia

hints.text: **Eucalyptus (gum) trees** everywhere, light bark; drives on the **left**
Bollard: white, **red reflector front / grey back** (NZ = red wraps around the back)
Almost all **white** road lines — yellow lines → South Africa or NZ instead
Give-way sign: white border + **black** text (NZ = red text); speed sign red circle on white rectangle (NZ = no rectangle)
Gen3 = long antenna; chevrons white-on-black & black-on-yellow
**Stobie pole** (two steel joists + concrete slab middle) is iconic to South Australia
Rusty metal poles w/ holes; **130 km/h** speed limit = NT only; termite mounds
**Yellow signposts**; black-and-white **stripy bollards** unique to WA; green-based poles
~**64% of concrete poles** are in Victoria; 'C' roads only in Victoria & Tasmania
Unique **pole-top coils** angled upward; fluorescent-border road signs; black-and-yellow bollard
**Red 'No Stopping' signs** (other states white); snorkel-fitted rare cars → NSW/ACT
Interior = **red soil**, dry; SE + SW corners greener; can "fry eggs" red outback
Tasmania: cool temperate green rolling hills (like NZ/S. Chile)
Coastal N. Queensland tropical: palms + sugarcane; Darwin (NT) dark red soil + black tree trunks
Different states use different **licence-plate** designs + slogans; 2-digit phone area codes map to state
Plates long white + EU strip (Italy: 2 strips & shorter; Switzerland: no strip)
White bollards w/ black top, **dark-red or dark-grey reflector**, often a nipple-shaped black cap (sometimes snow pole on top)

shared_with.diff: Bright red → Slovenia; dark red/grey → Austria.

shared_with.note: Same design w/ bright red reflector.
Germany = simple white bollards w/ grey reflectors, no black top.

hints.text: Chevrons **white-on-red or red-on-yellow** (Germany: mostly red-on-white)
**Round wooden poles** most common
Mostly mountainous + forested; east/northeast flatter & agricultural
**Alpine architecture**: light walls, wooden balconies/top-floor panelling, dark overhanging wooden roofs (S-Germany similar)
**German** (Ä Ö Ü ß); camera meta separates the German trio: Austria = Gen3 **and** Gen4; Germany = only Gen4; Switzerland = always low-cam

shared_with.diff: **Gen3 coverage → Austria** (Germany has none); Einbahnstraße vs EINBAHN.

hints.text: Direction + town-entry signs: **white w/ blue outline + unique font** (Germany: yellow town signs); green facility-direction signs common
Pedestrian sign: **two horizontal dashed lines** instead of zebra stripes (all neighbours use stripes)
hints.uniq_note: Belgium and Poland also use line-based designs — but within the Alpine neighbourhood this sign means Austria.

hints.text: Signs sometimes mounted with **4 metal clips** (unique in Central Europe); road markers **blue w/ white numbers + one decimal comma** (SI: white, DE: white outline); **EINBAHN** one-way signs (DE: Einbahnstraße); 'Anfang'/'Ende' on no-stopping signs; white upside-down-T dead-end signs
Bus stops: **yellow-green H circle** (only Germany similar)

shared_with.diff: Blue-outline town signs → Austria; yellow town signs → Germany.

hints.text: **Yellow natural-gas poles** (snow-pole lookalikes) — unique to Austria
Gen4 smallcam coverage (like most of Western Europe)

**Elevation = region**: central+west most mountainous; 'flat' east still rolling; tallest sharpest peaks → the panhandle on the Italian border; **vineyards only in the far east** (Vienna + Lake Neusiedl)
**A- and B-road numbers increase east → west** (exception: B222–230 = Vienna); state flags + per-state **bus companies** (logo on the bus)

**State metas**: Vorarlberg = neon-yellow bus-stop poles + yellow-white pole stripes + white km markers (also one W-Salzburg road); Salzburg = white painted square next to km markers; Burgenland = green 'Güterweg' signs; Upper Austria = white Güterweg w/ black outline; Lower Austria = **yellow-blue sign borders** (state flag)
**Tin-foil antenna** → western Austria near the Swiss border (Austria-unique)

**Vienna**: dark-blue plaques w/ white serif font — **leading number = district**; Salzburg = unique font; Klagenfurt = irregular white border; St. Pölten = rounded white border; Vienna+Innsbruck poles w/ **red-white flag tape** (Vienna: thinner, usually two per pole)
**Lake Constance** on the western border — vast, unlike any mountain lake; Gen2 only on one Burgenland road + ski trekkers
Autonomous region of Portugal → **Portuguese infrastructure** (poles, chevrons, bollards)

shared_with.diff: Volcanic rock walls + hydrangeas + rolling pasture → Azores.

hints.text: Mostly **open agricultural fields in a rolling landscape**, taller round hills in the background

shared_with.note: Madeira is very mountainous and rarely has open fields.
shared_with.diff: Rolling pasture → Azores; steep terraces → Madeira.

shared_with.note: Green pasture + stone walls can feel Irish.
shared_with.diff: Volcanic black rock + Portuguese signs → Azores.

hints.text: **Dry rock walls of volcanic rocks** very common
hints.uniq_note: Madeira barely has them; mainland Portugal's dry walls aren't volcanic black.

shared_with.note: Mainland dry walls exist but not from volcanic rock.
Dry rock walls are not common on Madeira.

hints.text: **Hydrangeas** commonly line roads; **Japanese cedars** (cryptomeria) very common — not native anywhere in mainland Europe

shared_with.note: Hydrangeas rare, cedars less common there.

hints.text: Houses 1–2 storeys, orange tiled roofs, white/pastel walls, often **dark-green shutters**; **grey highlights** almost exclusive to the Azores (mainland: yellow/blue)

shared_with.note: Yellow/blue highlights exist on the mainland too.

hints.text: Street signs almost exclusively **painted ceramic tiles**: blue letters + decorative pattern around the edge
hints.uniq_note: Ceramic azulejo-style street signs as the DEFAULT is an Azores tell (mainland has them only in old towns).

hints.text: Signposts often **striped** (also on Madeira)
**Black-and-white cows** very common

shared_with.note: Madeiran cows are usually brown with white spots.

hints.text: Only **São Miguel, Terceira and Faial** have car coverage; **Gen4 only on São Miguel**; shitcam on São Miguel (car w/ centre indentation) + Faial (smooth car, sometimes antenna)
**Lakes → only São Miguel** (in all of Azores + Madeira); rock walls w/ **white concrete top → Terceira**; whitewashed walls w/ uneven dark basalt stones → **Faial**
**Faial**: trekker walking along a crater edge
Plates white, black or **turquoise** — the turquoise plate exists only in Bangladesh
**Bengali**: continuous top line per word (like Devanagari) but many **triangular letters**

shared_with.note: Bengali is official in eastern India (West Bengal) too.
shared_with.diff: India = shitcam/smallcam + B&W signposts w/ Indian context; check the cars and plate colours.

hints.text: Gen3 cars: **red-mirror w/ bars**, **classic white**, **striped white** (only the classic white isn't Bangladesh-unique)
hints.uniq_note: Red-mirror and striped-white cars are unique to Bangladesh — but don't confuse the striped white with Cambodia's similar-looking car.

hints.text: Gen4: **black or white car**; Gen4 often has a **smoky feel**; shitcam exists too (black/white car, sometimes fully blurred)
**Rickshaws** everywhere — especially the green auto-rickshaws
**Black-white striped signposts**
**Water bodies + agricultural fields** right beside the roads; streets lined with trees, often **white-painted trunk bottoms**
**Red-and-white concrete bollards** countrywide (circular or square)
**Tin roofs** everywhere (common across humid South/SE Asia)

**Roof styles split east/west**: west (Rajshahi/Khulna/Dhaka) = sheet-metal **hip roofs**; east (Sylhet exclusively; also Chittagong, Mymensingh, E-Rangpur, N-Dhaka) = metal roofs w/ **triangular 'window'** below the ridge; tiled roofs only on the Khulna/India border (West-Bengal style)
**Rice growth stage = region** (Gen4 only!)
bullets: Early growth (short, in water): Khulna, Mymensingh, Dhaka S of Padma
Mid growth (taller, green): Rangpur, Barishal, Chittagong
Fully grown (tall, yellow/orange, fluffy): Rajshahi (+Rangpur, Chittagong)
Harvested (beige stubble): Sylhet + S-Khulna
Off-season (barren fields): Dhaka N of Padma
Vast empty rice plains: Dhaka/Mymensingh/Sylhet border

hints.text: Almost fully **flat** — exceptions: tall forested hills in E-Chittagong; E-W ridge on the Indian border seen from N-Sylhet/E-Mymensingh (often hazy)

**Crop metas**: corn → west (Rajshahi/Rangpur/W-Mymensingh/N-Khulna, small patches S-Barishal); jute (tall green; dried on fences/roads anywhere) → Rangpur; canola → Dhaka N of Padma; tea → S-Sylhet highlands; fruit orchards → Rajshahi/Rangpur near India; sunflowers + watermelons → S-Barishal; eucalyptus → north (Rangpur/Rajshahi/Mymensingh); blossoming trees (spring coverage) → Khulna/Mymensingh/S-Dhaka; beach sheoaks → Teknaf coast + Noakhali
**Copyright-year meta** (Gen4): 2022 → Mymensingh/Dhaka (+some Chittagong); 2023 → Rangpur/Rajshahi/Khulna/Barishal/Sylhet; 2024/25 everywhere
**Coverage metas**: shitcam only in/around Dhaka; Gen3 white car → Dhaka, Chattogram + connecting highway; Gen3 striped car → yellow roads west of Dhaka
Gen3: a **guard with a hayfork** often visible beside the car
Another **guard beside the car** (not the Sylhet one; the same man rarely appears on trekkers elsewhere)
**Trekker metas**: black-cover trekker → Khulna; red-mirror trekker → Nijhum Dwip (unique); scooter trekker (left mirror + hair) → Hatiya Island; security-guard escort → Saint Martin's Island
Only coverage (bar spills) = a **single walking trekker in historic central Minsk**, mostly pedestrian-only streets
Historic Minsk = well-kept **classical European architecture**, white/pastel walls
The **Holy Spirit Cathedral** (two towers) features prominently and can be seen from afar
Signs in **Belarusian or Russian** (both Cyrillic); letters **ў, і** are not in Russian

shared_with.note: Russian is used in Belarus too.
shared_with.diff: Letters ў / і → Belarusian, not Russian.

hints.text: Minsk-centre street signs are **sea-green** ("вул" = street); pavement mostly grey rectangular tiles (pink pattern section on one part)
Plates with **red text** — visible through the blur
White bollards w/ **yellow rectangular front reflector** (white back); dark-brown bollard w/ 2 red stripes (also NL)

shared_with.note: Similar white bollards — but with red stripe + non-rectangular reflector.
The brown 2-red-stripe bollard exists there too.

hints.text: A-type guardrails w/ **yellow reflectors** (Europe: only ES/AD/BE)
Concrete **square poles w/ tiny holes** + small metal wrappings (wrappings also in France); second type w/ oval holes
Directional signs blue or white; **small BLUE sub-signs** under road signs (other countries: white); km markers green (sometimes red text)
hints.uniq_note: The blue sub-sign is the quick Belgium tell — neighbours use white ones.

hints.text: Pedestrian sign: **two horizontal dotted lines + square-headed man** (FR/NL: 5-stripe zebra)
Chevrons **white w/ red arrow** (also NL, never FR)

shared_with.diff: Red plate text + concrete roads → Belgium.

hints.text: Rural roads often of **concrete slabs** (PH/TH-like; NL almost never); all-white lines; bike lanes both sides w/ **short dashed outer lines**; **gutters on both sides of almost every road** (rare next door)
hints.uniq_note: The gutters + concrete slabs combo is the fastest NL-vs-BE separator.

hints.text: **Red-brick houses** (N+W); south = grey stone, French look; **2nd-most blurred houses** after Germany

**Orange signposts/sign backs** — unique to Belgium; street signs short & tall, often w/ **municipality name** (NL signs long & low); town entry = white w/ black border + bold name; priority signs = small orange diamonds; bus stops = **yellow posts** + yellow shelter roofs
Traffic lights on **black-yellow or red-white striped poles**; red **H hydrant signs** (also Germany)
Rare red car w/ long antenna (much commoner in Ukraine); Gen4 smallcam
**3 languages**: Dutch/Flemish (z, w, ee, ij) → Flanders (north); French (ç é è à) → Wallonia (south); German (ä ü ö ß) → Ostbelgien; **Brussels bilingual FR+NL**

**Flanders vs Wallonia**: bus-stop top white (Flanders/Brussels) vs **yellow** (Wallonia); warning signs thick red border w/o white rim (Flanders, = Spanish style) vs w/ white rim (Wallonia); traffic-light poles **yellow-black = Flanders only**, red-white = Wallonia/Brussels
**Area codes**: 1–2 digits after the 0 (04 = mobile); big-city street-sign designs learnable (Bruges doesn't name the municipality!)
Flat NW (coast) → hillier SE; **Ardennes** = steep hills, dense forest, valleys (spills into FR/LU/DE); coastal plain = polders (NL-confusable!); coast lined by an apartment **'wall'** + dunes
**Baarle-Hertog** (NE of Antwerp): BE/NL enclave patchwork — borders marked with **white crosses**, street signs reveal the country
British overseas territory north of the Caribbean — **counts as UK** in country streaks; drives on the **left**
**Black Street View car** w/ the entire front sides typically visible
Typical look: fairly dense vegetation of **small trees + many bushes**, single-family houses strewn across; **palms** very common; the ocean is never far and looks distinctly **light blue**
Plates **all-white, fairly long**, black digits bunched in the middle
Most roads: **single solid yellow middle line, NO outer lines**; occasional white **zig-zag lines**
hints.uniq_note: Left-hand traffic + lone yellow centre line is a Bermuda signature.

hints.text: **Pastel stone houses w/ completely WHITE roofs** (stepped, for rain catchment)
**Hamilton**: by far the largest town — dense multi-floor downtown
**NW tip** (Royal Naval Dockyard): many large **unpainted grey-brick buildings**
**Boat trekker** on the east coast: around Saint George's Harbour + past Fort St. Catherine (NE tip)
Plates almost always **full red**; taxis = **yellow plates + yellow roofs**
hints.uniq_note: Within Asia, Bhutan is the only country with full-red plates — outside Asia, red plates exist (e.g. some European trade plates), so confirm with Dzongkha script or the stone barriers.

hints.text: Coverage = **grey Google car without antenna**
hints.uniq_note: The only LEFT-driving country with a grey car — don't mistake it for a white car (e.g. Philippines' silvery grey drives right).

hints.text: **Stone-block safety barriers** on winding rural roads (guardrails rare) — the best rural Bhutan tell
Traditional architecture: **tin roofs + ornate painted wooden frames** — very distinct
**Dzongkha** script — on Street View these letters exist only in Bhutan
Mountainous — coverage mostly on main mountain roads (flat areas only in the south); **prayer flags** along roads
Thin metal poles, often **painted black at the bottom**
Road markers: **yellow top + white bottom** — best rural pinpointing clue; national-highway numbers on top (not on the map — learnable table)
Red/blue **city signs with the town name** — instant location tell in cities
**Black-white striped signposts**
Coverage on main roads & cities; **two solid white outer lines** → only western Bhutan near/south of Thimphu; flat landscape → only the four southern regions
**Phobjikha Valley** (S of Nubding): the largest wide, flat agricultural valley with no major river in view
A **hook on the back of the car** in Haa District (mainly the town of Ha) — sometimes barely visible
**Late-sunset coverage** in exactly two places: rural sideroad in east Mongar + urban area around Paro
**Airport metas**: Gelephu = the only southern flat airport; Paro International = dry NW valley; Bumthang Domestic = wet northern valley
Plates **short, white w/ blue text** — blue often invisible through the blur (looks plain white)
**Only Gen3 coverage** — solid white car, no antenna

shared_with.note: Same white antenna-less car — but Peru HAS Gen4.

shared_with.diff: Chile's black-section variant + all-white/all-yellow lines → Chile.

hints.text: Buildings of **large red bricks w/ an 'unfinished' look**

shared_with.note: Common near Lake Titicaca in Peru too.
shared_with.diff: White square signposts + Tigo ads → Bolivia.

hints.text: Middle lines **all-yellow, yellow+white mixed, or all-white**; outer lines always white

shared_with.note: Peru often has outer-lines-only roads — very rare in Bolivia.

hints.text: Lots of **concrete-slab roads** + **cobblestone roads** w/ neat stone lines separating disorganised cobbles
**Bulky white square signposts**
Guardrail markings w/ **yellow stripes on the OUTSIDE**

shared_with.note: Peru inverts it: black on the outside.
shared_with.diff: Yellow outer → Bolivia; black outer → Peru.

hints.text: Poles inconsistent: round concrete w/ pinholes + wooden (rarely Argentina-style); **extremely curvy tree-trunk wooden poles** (esp. east) = a very good Bolivia clue
hints.uniq_note: Slightly bent poles exist elsewhere — EXTREMELY curvy ones scream Bolivia.

hints.text: km markers: **black top w/ 'Km' / white bottom w/ distance — NO road number**

shared_with.note: Similar from afar, but Argentina's markers show a road number.

hints.text: **Traditional dress** common (bowler hats, colourful shawls, pollera skirts — also near-border Peru); **MAS/Evo political graffiti** + objects painted black-white-blue; **Wiphala** flag (7-colour diagonal patchwork); **Tigo** telecom ads (does NOT operate in Peru — solves urban 50-50s); rocks & soil often distinctly **reddish**
hints.uniq_note: MAS colours + Wiphala + Tigo are Bolivia's fastest urban tells vs Peru.

hints.text: Coverage very limited: only central/central-west — six main cities (**La Paz/El Alto, Cochabamba, Oruro, Sucre, Potosí, Santa Cruz**) have side streets; elsewhere main roads only (often partly unpaved)

**Altiplano** plateau dominates the west: grasslands w/ tufted 'hedgehog' grass, mostly flat roads w/ hills around; **lusher north (El Alto/La Paz), arid south (Oruro→Chile)** — exception: one side of divided Ruta 1 looks green up to Oruro; **Lake Titicaca** visible from Ruta 1 (Desaguadero↔Guaqui, lake north) + Ruta 2 (Tiquina↔Batallas, lake S/W), very green around

**Andes** (NW-SE) = tall mountains between Altiplano & Amazon; **east of the Andes** = green tropical flat farmland w/ characteristic **pink soil** (unpaved coverage only around Santa Cruz); **cobblestone roads** mainly in/SE of Cochabamba + around Sucre
**Cities**:
bullets: La Paz: hilly, meandering green valleys; downtown = the ONLY 10+ storey towers; yellow-beige eroded sandstone pillars on hillsides (only here)

El Alto: flat plateau, dry & dusty, grid streets, endless unfinished red brick; wavy-edged grey brick pavement; **Neo-Andean** futuristic colourful facades (almost only here); gradient-painted gates (green→light green, red→yellow)
Cochabamba: flat valley w/ an imposing mountain WALL to the north
Oruro: driest big-city landscape, large dry hill to the west
Sucre: very hilly + round tree-covered hills; historic white-wall colonial centre w/ orange tiles
Potosí: hilly + arid, treeless hills; giant pyramid mountain **Cerro Rico** (mining-stripped)
Santa Cruz: the only big city in flat tropical surroundings (nearby towns look identical)

hints.text: **Road spotlights**:
bullets: Ruta 1 west of El Alto: paved through green grass, white paint numbers on the road, Titicaca north near Desaguadero
Ruta 3 near Pongo (NE of La Paz): enormous dark-grey mountains + low clouds
**Death Road** (N Yungas): bendy unpaved jungle road, foggy south, cyclists — the only jungle dirt road
Ruta 1/4 La Paz↔Oruro: the ONLY large divided highway
Ruta 4 (Vila Vila→Cochabamba→Buena Vista): square smudge front-left
Morochata/Ruta 25: isolated Peru-lookalike mountain dirt road (eucalyptus west, arid east)
Ruta 1 Machacamarca↔Guindal: snow patches + gloomy overcast (north of Challapata: straight NNW, better sky; SE of Challapata: curvier, greyer)
Ruta 7 Mairana↔Santa Cruz: neon-like camera glitch to the north
Mataral area (Ruta 7/22): green low-vegetation hills, sunny; big cacti + yellow-flowered trees
Eastern Ruta 4 (E of Santa Cruz): flat green E-W, asphalt + concrete pads
Ruta 5 near Chaco (N of Sucre): sunset coverage on concrete
Ruta 4 SW of Patacamaya: worn grainy asphalt in semi-arid grass
Nevado Sajama (lone snow volcano) visible → SW Ruta 4 south of Callapa
Ruta 6 Tarabuco↔Zudáñez: near-white concrete (unique)
Villa Vitalina↔Coipasa: salt-flat + volcanic-cone desert drive

hints.text: **Sajama NP**: unique car w/ black roof rack + metal bars, unpaved road w/ fluffy bushes, Nevado Sajama always visible; Tomarapi village = adobe walls + thatched roofs
**White car, no antenna** (in Africa also ZA / Lesotho / Eswatini)
Plates **white front / yellow rear** (UK-style) — only Botswana & Namibia in southern Africa predominantly have yellow rear plates
Signs in **English**; town names from indigenous languages; **Afrikaans very rare** (unlike South Africa)
Landscape **dry & mostly flat**: shrubs, small trees, golden grass; sandy soil white → red
Road lines **yellow outer / white middle** (Southern Africa; note Botswana drives **left**)
Unique **black-and-yellow striped signposts** (in S. Africa only Botswana); striped kerbs & guardrails
Green direction signs; **road numbers start with A** (South Africa never starts with A); some older **blue-background** signs (~unique to Botswana)
Houses range shack → bungalow, exposed grey brick, occasional round huts; **random small blurs** common in coverage
Coverage sparse (densest in the east + near Gaborone); mostly **pancake-flat**, hills only in ~2 areas (SE between Gaborone & Lobatse)

videos.title: 3 Levels of GeoGuessr Tips from a Pro - BRAZIL
How to recognize Brazil in GeoGuessr

hints.text: Plates: white until 2018, now **white w/ blue top stripe**; red = commercial vehicles
**Portuguese** (not Spanish): **-ão** instead of -ón, **-ch/-lh** instead of -ll, **-nh** instead of -ñ
hints.uniq_note: Within the Americas only Brazil speaks Portuguese.

hints.text: **Distinctly red soil** in most of the country — light pink (Mato Grosso) to deep maroon (Paraná river); NOT red in the northeast or most of Rio Grande do Sul

shared_with.note: Misiones province has deep red soil too.

hints.text: Most common poles look like a **ladder at the bottom**: rectangular, long segments below, small holes near the top
hints.uniq_note: Paraguay uses similar ladder poles — Portuguese vs Spanish decides.

hints.text: Sign backs mostly **painted black** (also common in Paraguay); **blue road markers** w/ road number + km on roadsides
Chevrons **yellow-on-black**
hints.uniq_note: Most of South America uses the inverted black-on-yellow.

hints.text: Usually **double yellow middle + white outer lines** (occasionally single dashed yellow); **white-painted kerbs** frequent
Many houses w/ **orange tiled roofs**; **blue Fortlev water tanks** on roofs; **transparent satellite dishes** (within LatAm generally Brazil-only)
hints.uniq_note: The transparent dish is the strong tell; tanks/roofs support it.

hints.text: Gen3: white car w/ **stubby coiled antenna** OR white car w/ no antenna (often only the antenna or nothing visible); Gen4 cars w/ visible front: **blue**, **white w/ blue stripe**, or **white**; smallcam exists (in S. America only Brazil, Peru, Argentina)
Coverage concentrated in the **eastern half**; Amazon very sparse — build a mental map of the few western roads
**Area codes (DDD)** in parentheses, grouped by first digit; codes ending in **1 = major city/state capital**; numbers w/o code: 8 digits landline / 9 mobile
**2-letter state acronyms** on state-road numbers (km markers etc.) — learnable by heart; **CEP postcodes** on street signs: first 2 digits run **anti-clockwise from São Paulo** to Rio Grande do Sul
**Landscape by region**:
bullets: Elevation: highest near the SE coast (map)
Northeast = Caatinga: dry soil, shrubs, succulents (deceptively green in rainy season)
Amazon: dense rainforest or grassy hills w/ sparse trees; few roads
Acre: small grass hills, roads RIDE the hills (also near Marabá, Pará)
Roraima: open grassy plains + big distant mountains; thin bunched grass
Rio Grande do Sul: lush green fields, hilly or flat (south resembles Uruguay)
MG/RJ/ES: rolling grassy hills; ES + surroundings: big exposed-rock mountains

hints.text: **Flora**:
bullets: Paraná pines (long horizontal branches, leaves only at the tips): PR, SC, RS
Coconut palms: NE states + coast
Carnaúba palms (fan leaves, spiked lower trunk): CE, PI, RN (+ Xique Xique, BA)
Babassu palms (long robust leaves): Amazon
Açaí palms (drooping leaflets, very thin trunks): around Belém/Amazon
Moriche palms (rounded fans, dead leaves drooping): RR, AM, TO, GO
Macaw palms (thin leaves, spear top): MS, N-TO, around Belo Horizonte — use multiple trees
Long-stemmed leafy plants: CE, RN, PB
Very tall grass: TO, GO, BA, MG

hints.text: **Agriculture**: eucalyptus plantations everywhere (esp. south + Amapá); sugarcane → São Paulo by far (+ PR, MS, NE coast); soybeans → MT, PR, GO; coffee → Minas Gerais (+ ES, NE-SP)
**Pole types & IDs by state**:
bullets: Round concrete poles: south, esp. São Paulo (+ big-city centres everywhere)
Wooden poles: RS, Roraima, Amazonas, RJ
Crossbar supports common (rare in the NE); single support → TO, SC, MG
Yellow paint ID starting w/ a LETTER → BA & PE; starting w/ a NUMBER → GO
Small yellow/orange plate on thin ties → RN & PE (higher up in PE)
Black paint + yellow/white text → Paraná
Beige plate built into the pole → MA (rarer PA/PI)
Street name painted on poles at intersections → Paraná (spills into SC/RS)
Asymmetric crossbar + support to the long side → SP (also RS, RJ, MS)
Asymmetric crossbar w/o supports → only CE (concrete) & RO (metal, rare)
Zigzag top bar on flat crossbar → only CE & RR
'Pencil poles' (smooth cone top) → RJ (rare Brasília/Manaus)
Γ-shaped poles w/ electricity box → RJ + Belém/Salvador/Fortaleza/Maceió
Electricity counters ON poles → Cuiabá, Macapá, PA cities
1 top insulator + alternating horizontals → MT + S-Acre

hints.text: **Insulators by state**:
bullets: Brown mushrooms → only Pernambuco
White mushrooms → Bahia (rare Brasília)
Transparent → Alagoas + Santa Catarina (rare SE/CE/DF)
Thread-spool shape → RJ & ES (seen in CE)
Uniquely long & thin → Ceará (rare GO)
Horizontal insulators → Pará (less MT; rare AC/AP/TO/SC/RS)
Long grey → PA, MT, TO, BA, MS, PR, SC
Black double-UFO trident → Maranhão configuration
'Lollipop' (sphere on a rod) → RN, PB, PE (rare SE)
Dark brown w/ grey base → SC & RS

hints.text: **Lamps & misc**: concrete trash baskets → AC/RO (metal elsewhere); two-part lamps → MG/GO; arm-less fancy lamps → NW-Paraná; lamp arm attached directly to pole → MT/MS/PA/AP/RN/PE/SC; Roraima lamp colours: red = Boa Vista/Normandia/Mucajaí, blue = Rorainópolis, green = Pacaraima/Iracema, yellow = Caracaraí; colourful ornate lamp arms (often w/ town name) → Ceará; **João-de-Barro nests** (mud spheres on poles) → south

**Roads**: São Paulo has by far the most divided highways (+ phone numbers under km markers); cobblestone → NE + RS; hexagonal concrete tiles → SC & MG; red-brick roads → Acre; random-stone cobble → SE-RS & MG; uneven square stones + dark soil → W-RS/W-SC (same in Misiones, AR); brown-tile pavements → RS; red roadside gravel → Piauí (caution)
**Architecture**: European (vaguely German) style → SC & the south; wooden stilt houses → Acre (rarer low-lying AM/PA)
**Amapá**: ALL Gen4 has a **smudge front-right** + always the white car w/ blue stripe; Gen3 **rifts → Alagoas**; NW-RS has a distinct Gen3 camera glitch

**Recognisable roads**: BR-364 (Acre) = rollercoaster road riding the hills, no traffic, blue Gen4 car; BR-319 (Amazonas) = lush flat mostly-unpaved Amazon road w/ bright orange soil + poles all along; BR-163 (Pará) = hilly, curvy, dry/hazy, truck traffic (blue car north, smallcam south)
**Landscape spotlights**: Uiramutã (N-Roraima) = dry mountains w/ distinctive 'folds'; coastal RS = rare flat wetland

**City metas**: SP yellow square pole ID (3 municipality letters + numbers); wrapped sticker street signs → Campo Grande (w/ red-yellow stripe) & Joinville; fully-curved lamp arms → Rio de Janeiro; blue lamps w/ orange arm → Teresina; orange lamp heads → Belém; light-green lamps + multi-counter poles → Cuiabá; white numbered lamp plaques → Belo Horizonte (0/1) & Uberaba (1/2); zigzag pole + yellow head → Caxias do Sul; poles white w/ blue+red stripes → Florianópolis

**Trekkers & unique cars**: Amazon boat+walking trekker on the Madeira/Aripuanã rivers (rainforest + villages); Lençóis Maranhenses NP = sand dunes w/ water pools, white truck w/ black bed; **Fernando de Noronha** (340 km offshore): white buggy visible at the front, big exposed rocks + boat coverage
UK overseas territory in the Indian Ocean (counts as UK); all coverage = a **Gen3 trekker**, none pinpointable (rare in community maps)
Mostly small **atolls in the northern half**; much coverage on **sand beaches lined w/ palms**
Coastal **coconut-palm forests**; **mangroves** on several atolls; grassy fields w/ bushes on the larger islands; trekker sometimes offshore in the water
**Coin du Mire** = dark rocky shore + grassy field (no palms); a white shipwreck sits on the beach at the E tip of **Nelsons Island**
Plates standard European (blue strip left)
**Cyrillic — but very often paired with Latin** transliterations; **Ъ** only Bulgarian & (rarely) Russian; street = улица (ул.)

shared_with.diff: Latin next to Cyrillic + EU plates → Bulgaria; frequent Ъ as a vowel → Bulgarian.
Macedonian Ѕ Ј Љ Њ → North Macedonia.

hints.text: Gen4 cars: blue (pan-European), **red** (also LV/LT/RU/SK/SE) or black (also GR/HR) — ± antenna, sometimes hidden; Gen3 = mostly **March/April dead-'winter'** look + grey-tape **'flag antenna'**
Cylindrical concrete poles (+ladder variant, wooden); most poletops = small **alternating hook insulators**; **3 large upward-hook insulators ≈ unique to Bulgaria** (downward variant exists)
hints.uniq_note: The 3-large-hook poletop is 'probably unique' per Plonk It — the small alternating hooks are the everyday version.

hints.text: Chevrons red-on-white (also RO/TR/MK; GR/RS = black-white); directional + town signs show **two lines (Cyrillic + Latin)** — visible even from afar
hints.uniq_note: The two-line bilingual signage is the fast Bulgaria tell among Cyrillic countries.

hints.text: Pedestrian sign: **4 stripes + person wearing a hat** — no other Eastern/Central-European sign has 4 stripes
Bollards **rare** & generic (red front/white back, inconsistent shape — ≈ HR/HU designs)
**Thin road lines** (Romania: much thicker); road quality fairly poor (cracks, potholes)
Orange tiled roofs w/ **mortar patches along corner tiles** — famously uneven, 'poorly built' look
hints.uniq_note: The sloppy mortar-corner roofs are the classic Bulgaria architecture tell.

hints.text: **Metallic trash bins** of a distinct shape (also TR/RS/AL/MK)
**Balkan range splits the country**: flat north to Romania; **Rhodope** SE w/ highest peaks; second flat area between the ranges; west = small valleys in hills
**Area codes ordered by first digit** — the single best urban region clue
**Pirin & Rila NP**: pyramid peaks visible from Blagoevgrad/Kyustendil/Sofia provinces; wind turbines → Dobrich coast; Alpine-look houses → Smolyan region

**Car metas**: invisible Gen4 car → N+W borders + northern coast; red Gen4 → central + SE; short tape antenna → east (never Vratsa/Montana/Pazardzhik/Smolyan); taped LONG antenna + white car → Stara Zagora (+rare Plovdiv/Burgas); bulky unpeeled-tape antenna + March coverage → **Varna**
**Vratsa**: distinct 'bumpy' mountains SW of the city; **Nessebar/Sozopol** old towns = unpainted stone + white walls + brown wood + cobblestone
**Sofia**: white street signs w/ two red strips (others exist); **Stara Zagora**: brown signs w/ bright yellow text
Plates white with **blue letters** (may look plain white through the blur); government vehicles = **green plates**
Gen3 cars: **stubby 3-ridge antenna** car OR **striped white car** (white stripe left, yellow right)
hints.uniq_note: The striped white car is unique to Cambodia (Bangladesh's striped car looks slightly different). The stubby antenna resembles Latin-American cars (MX/CO/EC/BR) — theirs have a diagonally coiled ridge instead of three ridges.

hints.text: Drives on the **right**

shared_with.diff: Latin script w/ heavy diacritics → Vietnam.
Lao script (Thai-like) → Laos; Khmer is far more intricate.

hints.text: **Khmer script** — very distinct, intricate curvy letters (font-dependent)

shared_with.note: Thai looks similar but less intricate.
shared_with.diff: Left-hand traffic + tiny circles in letters → Thailand.
Simplified look + Lao context (yellow regular plates) → Laos.

hints.text: Poles: concrete **ladder poles** (indents like a ladder) + round concrete poles w/ small holes (rarely wooden)

shared_with.note: Thai poles are **square with pinhole rows** — very uncommon in Cambodia.

hints.text: Common bollards: stone w/ **red-white stripes** or **red top + white base** (reflector in the red part); some are road/region-specific
Road markers: stone, white base + **rounded red top** — road number on the narrow side, town+distance on the main side
Chevrons **black with yellow arrows** (rare; bollards often used instead)
hints.uniq_note: All other SE-Asian countries use the reverse scheme (yellow with black arrows) — black chevrons in SEA mean Cambodia.

hints.text: **Yellow middle lines** (dashed/solid); outer lines rare, white when present; many roads **unpaved**

**Wooden stilt houses** with vertical plank walls, double/triple roofs; walls dark red-brown / light blue / ochre, details painted **bright blue**; tiled orange/red roofs w/ ridge ornaments or metal roofs
Majority **Buddhist** — decorative temples + Buddhist flags (Thailand/Laos temples look similar)
Beer ads everywhere: **Angkor, Anchor, 'Cambodia Beer'**; blue **CPP party signs** (often three men side by side)
**Shitcam** exists (wide circular blur, brownish) — also in India/Bangladesh/Sri Lanka/Nepal; here only in the **southwestern half**
Coverage limited — mostly around **Phnom Penh, Siem Reap, Battambang**
**Central plain** = flat cultivated farmland w/ villages; **northeast** = uncultivated, natural, slightly hilly; **southwest** = hills & low mountains, dense inland forest, populated coast
**White sandy soil** → SE of Phnom Penh + around the city; near the **Thai border**: mountain ridge to the north + red soil (flat roads below the abrupt drop-off)
**Rubber plantations** (thin trunks, taps, neat rows): belt NE of Phnom Penh (Roads 7/71), around Ban Lung + Road 78, around Pech Sreada (dry-season look + very orange soil)

**Bollard spotlights**: black-white bollards ONLY on Road 48 (Tatai↔Road 4 — Thai-style, verify country first!); unique **triangular red-striped bollards** on Road 4 (Angk Snuol↔Preah Sihanouk, esp. after Kirirom), a Ream-NP offroad + Preah Monivong NP

**Road spotlights (SW)**: Road 48→Kiri Sakor under construction (one paved side, red soil); jungle offroads NE of Road 48 (red soil, dense jungle, hilly — eastern lookalikes have browner soil); Phnum Samkos: overcast + big distant mountains, brown unpaved roads, wild green; Road 4 has a **wide red-brown shoulder** (lighter near Phnom Penh); oil palms only on Road 4 north of Preah Nob
Highland plateau: **white rocky soil** + stunted windswept dark-green bushes — very recognisable

**Road spotlights (N/E)**: Road 41 SW of Phnom Penh — wide grey gravelly surface, runs NNE, flat farmland w/ distant hills (angle is key); Roads 6/5 (Siem Reap→Sisophon→Battambang): unique **3-beam poles w/ one-sided hanging insulators** + indented bases (west of Sisophon: insulators both sides); Road 76 south = overcast, dark crisp asphalt, outer lines, jungle hills; Road 76 north (Lumphat↔Mayura) = wide unpaved brown, semi-open jungle, skies clear toward the north
Bilingual signs with **Chinese** writing are common
Massive Khmer temple complex N of Siem Reap — dark weathered stone, extensive **trekker coverage** (huge presence in the official map)

videos.title: Never Mix Up The US and Canada Again
How to tell USA & Canada apart

hints.text: Speed signs say **'MAXIMUM'** in **km/h** — numbers higher than US ones, readable from afar; 'MAXIMUM' fits one line ('SPEED LIMIT' takes two)

shared_with.note: US signs say 'SPEED LIMIT' in miles, often ending in 5.

hints.text: Signs **less wordy** than the US: railroad crossbucks & yield signs typically **blank**
**British spelling** (centre, colour) + **metric system**
**Diamond-shaped checkerboard T-intersection signs** — the only country in the world with these
Usually **single yellow centre line** (double yellow decently common though)

shared_with.note: Double yellow is more common in the US.
shared_with.diff: Not 100% reliable — combine with other clues.

hints.text: Fair amount of **Gen2** (esp. rural/remote): big circular blur + halo-like sky distortion; Gen3 much more common than in the US, side-mirror blur = **two peaks** (US: single peak)

shared_with.note: Gen2 is very rare in the US (except Alaska/Hawaii).
shared_with.diff: North American Gen2 → lean Canada; two-peak mirror → Canada.

hints.text: **Communal mailboxes** (very rare in the US); Canada Post logo is **red** (USPS = blue)

shared_with.note: USPS blue logo; hardly any communal boxes.

hints.text: **North America vs Europe tells** (US + Canada): post mailboxes w/ flag; wooden poles + cylindrical metal transformers (never Europe); raised single insulator; short plates w/o blue strip; overhead stoplight bars; holed/U-profile signposts; tall water towers w/ town names; Gen4 smallcam w/ right mirror visible

Each province = own **plate design** (some readable blurred); several provinces don't require **front plates** (NB dropped them 2019 but older coverage still shows them; NL commercial vehicles keep front plates)
**French majority in Québec + parts of New Brunswick**; federal bilingualism → occasional bilingual signs anywhere (French communities also in NS/ON/MB)
Each province has its own **highway shield**; the green **Trans-Canada** shield appears in all 10 provinces; **km markers** vary by province — Ontario's have a unique **white square at the bottom**
Speed-sign variants: Ontario = black bottom section w/ 'km/h' or 'BEGINS'; BC = 'km/h' without black section; Québec sometimes French text
**Pole & transformer metas**:
bullets: Transformer faces the road → BC & New Brunswick (elsewhere parallel to wires)
Transformer at 45° → PEI & Nova Scotia (PEI: red numbers on the side)
Insulators facing to/away from road → QC, NS, PEI (elsewhere parallel; avoid intersections)
Ontario poletop: 2 side insulators w/ distinct attachment + 1 on top (stackable)
Québec: insulator rods angled slightly up; vertical black-yellow 'Q' label
Saskatchewan: insulators ALTERNATE 1-left/2-right ↔ 2-left/1-right (rarely MB)
Manitoba: two hanging insulators
PEI: small white pole sticker

hints.text: **Road-line metas**: double yellow in the East → most common Nova Scotia, then QC/PEI (Ontario prefers single); SK sometimes has **white dashes between the yellows**; centre rumble strips → AB & BC
Cones & chevrons: Ontario = **orange-black striped cones**; Québec = **orange-white cones** + the only province w/ **red-white chevrons** (rest of Canada: yellow-black)

3-digit **area codes** by region (7-digit numbers omit them); **provincial flags** appear often (Québec's Fleurdelisé especially); **Acadian flag** (French tricolour + yellow star) → N/E New Brunswick + pockets of NS/PEI
**Landscape regions**:
bullets: High mountains only in BC, western Alberta, Yukon
Yukon: boreal hills, conifers shrinking northward, lots of Gen2
BC interior (Kamloops/Kelowna): arid brown grasslands + pine hillsides
Great Plains: flat farmland, few trees, driest toward the Montana border
Canola (bright yellow fields): central-southern prairies (also North Dakota!)
NW Québec (Rouyn-Noranda): N-S grid, fields between forests, thin birches
S Saskatchewan: barren hilly river valleys (rest of the prairies = flat)
Aspens (white birch-like trunks, bright yellow in fall): MB/SK/AB forests
Canadian Shield (east): exposed rock everywhere
Québec City–Windsor corridor: densest population, flat green patchwork (looks like the US Great Lakes)
PEI: distinct RED soil + potato farms (occasionally NB/NS)
Newfoundland: Christmas-tree-like balsam firs

hints.text: **Bollard**: cylindrical black & white w/ 2 thin black bands — very common at intersections; band between can be yellow, rarely blue (Calgary) or green (Edmonton)
hints.uniq_note: NWT uses the same shape with THICKER bands; BC's version is thinner with wider band spacing.

hints.text: Rural roads numbered **sequentially in a grid**: Twp Rd = E–W (numbers rise northward), Rge Rd = N–S (rise westward, reset a few times) → near-exact location from one sign (similar naming rarely in SK); rare wire guardrails (posts + 3–4 metal wires) on major highways

Thin, slightly curved black-white **bollard** (esp. Vancouver Island); **concrete barriers** instead of guardrails; signs can have **green backsides** (also PEI); signs sometimes hung from unique posts
Bollards black & grey, usually w/ an **orange stripe below the grey**; wooden rectangular version further north; plain black-top version → MB & SK; **green-white striped signpost sticker**
Rural **grid roads named by number + cardinal**: 'Road 105N' = 105 miles north of the US border; E/W numbers count from the principal meridian just west of Winnipeg
Bollard cylindrical w/ 2 **thick** black bands (Alberta's are thinner)
**Diamond roadside markers** (colours vary); guardrails of cylindrical wooden posts + exactly **3 metal wires** (some posts w/ white reflective band)
**'Arrêt' stop signs = Québec only**; 'Stop/Arrêt' bilingual → most commonly New Brunswick (NB street signs bilingual too); QC T-intersection signs lack the checkerboard pattern

Long thin white **bollards attached to guardrails** w/ red or green top reflector; **yellow fire-hydrant markers** on signposts (Québec City: blue rectangle; Trois-Rivières: small; beware Edmundston NB); rectangular roadside markers (rarely NB divided highways)
**White triangles on signposts** (count varies)
Rectangular **lampheads** (also NS); Nova Scotia lamp arms = short & curved; flat **yellow bollards** on the NB Trans-Canada Highway
Guardrail posts w/ **pyramid-shaped tops**; diamond markers next to fire hydrants; **wooden sign boxes**; octagonal wood-plank bins on driveways; T-intersection signs w/ a yellow rectangular border
**Road spotlights**:
bullets: Dempster Hwy (YT-5/NWT-8 → Inuvik): unpaved Gen2, dark soil, tundra w/ short trees; flat + cloudier near Inuvik
Top of the World Hwy (W of Dawson City): overcast Gen2 high in the mountains
Hwy 1 near Beaver Creek (Yukon): group of motorcyclists ahead of the car
AB-93 (Banff/Jasper): steep sharp-peaked mountains both sides + thin tall trees
Drumheller (AB): treeless canyon-like rock formations
Trans-Labrador Hwy (500/510): sunny Gen4, thin pines, remote northern feel, low sun; snow near Happy Valley-Goose Bay
QC-167/QC-113: overcast Gen3 autumn, often snow + a lens smudge
Gaspé Peninsula inland: dense forested mountains, overcast
Cape Breton Highlands (NE NS): unusually much elevation, mixed forest
NL Route 430: SSW-NNE, washed-out lines, coast to the west, balsam firs + distant mountain wall east

hints.text: **Remote towns & trekkers**:
bullets: Iqaluit (NU capital): urban, turquoise bilingual street signs, bilingual speed signs
Churchill (N-MB): trekker w/ thin antenna front-right + unique street signs
Tulita (NWT): unique Google car + dark soil
Sanikiluaq (Hudson Bay): snowmobile, driver in dark brown coat visible
Inukjuak (N-QC): snowmobile visible through blur, NO driver (driver visible = Sanikiluaq)
Grise Fiord: northernmost covered town — unique mountains + ice floes
Resolute: barren w/ snow patches; Cambridge Bay: only NU Gen2 trekker
Qikiqtarjuaq: distinct mountains + red-tinted soil
Kangiqsualujjuaq (N-QC): green rocky hills, trees in the distance
Nain (Labrador): colourful wooden houses, big hill north, cliffs south, kids beside the trekker
Red Bay (S-Labrador): coast always visible, slightly red soil, white houses
Ellesmere Island: the world's northernmost coverage — glacier in barren rock (appears in competitive maps!)

hints.text: Road lines **all white OR all yellow, never mixed**: all-white most common; all-yellow in national parks / high-snowfall areas
hints.uniq_note: All-yellow lines are essentially Chile-only in the Americas; all-white also occurs in Argentina.

shared_with.note: Argentina has a decent amount of all-white-lined roads.
shared_with.diff: Yellow-black chevrons + square poles → Chile; white-red chevrons → Argentina.

hints.text: Gen3: **solid white car w/o antenna** — plain, or w/ a visible **black section (unique to Chile)**; Gen4 = blue car

shared_with.note: Plain white antenna-less Gen3 car there too.

shared_with.diff: Black section → Chile; red-brick towns → Bolivia.

hints.text: **Square concrete poles w/ an indent on both sides** + small pinholes up top (fewer horizontal supports than Brazil's ladder poles); wooden poles fairly common too

shared_with.note: Brazilian ladder poles look similar but have more supports in the indent.

hints.text: Regular plates white; **commercial & taxis = orange**

shared_with.note: Brazilian commercial plates are red — can look similar in some light.

hints.text: **Slots at the top of signposts**; chevrons **yellow w/ black arrows** (Argentina: white-red)
Bollards Spanish-style (white front reflector / yellow-orange back) but rare; more often the **orange temporary bollard w/ two white top stripes**; bridges often have **red-painted fence posts**
4000+ km long → **climate = latitude**: arid desert north, Mediterranean centre (Santiago), cold wet oceanic far south (Köppen map)
**Road naming: first letter runs A→Y from north to south** — extremely useful; 2-digit **area codes** regionally memorable (provinces differ in the centre)
**Landscape north→south**:
bullets: Atacama (northern third): extreme desert, often zero vegetation (extends into Peru/Bolivia/NW-Argentina)
Arica y Parinacota: smooth sandy eroded/bulbous mountains + valleys
Rocky uneven mountains: Atacama + N-Coquimbo; brown-red soil in Atacama (Gen3)
SW-Valparaíso: eucalyptus + dry climate
Around Santiago: semi-arid dry hills, scattered vegetation
Maule: greener Mediterranean + vineyards
Bío Bío: radiata pines w/ puffy needles; narrow tree-lined roads (also Araucanía)
Far-E Araucanía: monkey-puzzle trees (also across the border in Argentina)
Isla Grande de Chiloé: rolling NZ-like farmland + Chilean rhubarb (giant cabbage look)
S-Los Lagos/N-Aysén: lush green valleys, rocky mountains, overcast
S-Aysén: autumn colours in much coverage; dotted road lines → Los Ríos & Aysén

hints.text: **Recognisable roads**: A-31 = high altitude w/ steep valley drop north + Gen3 sunset; Ruta 21 (Calama→Chiu Chiu) = Gen4 sunset w/ pink-glowing volcanoes; B-710 & Ruta 5 = more Gen4 sunset; **Ruta 1** = coast squeezed between mountain wall (east) & Pacific (west); Ruta 41 = valley + snowy peaks + triple yellow lines; purple lupines → Ruta 240; **all-yellow lines on concrete → Magallanes** (barren grassy treeless far south)

**Nature parks**: Lauca = snow-capped line + 2 symmetrical lone volcanoes, fresh dark roads; Pampa del Tamarugal = Tamarugo trees along a NW road; Alto Loa = salt pans → snowy volcanoes; Los Flamencos = smooth reddish mountains + salt pans + triple yellow; Nevado Tres Cruces = red sand, salt pans, mountain pass; Llanos de Challe = brown dirt + juniper + overcast (Pan de Azúcar similar, barer); Alto Bío Bío = treeless cold + triple yellow + monkey puzzles east; Puyehue = dense vegetation, tar snakes, dead trees east

**Cities**: Arica = gentle sandy ridge; Iquique = towering ridge; Antofagasta = rocky uneven ridge; San Pedro de Atacama = clay-brick walls w/ triangle tops; orange street signs → Lanco (+ Malalhue); Villa O'Higgins = big mountains right east + orange-stained wood everywhere

**Lamp & pole city metas**: orange lamps → Calama & Chañaral; green dome lamps → Copiapó; photocell lamps → Santiago; grey flat-top lamps → Talca; blue lamps w/ yellow stripe → Concepción; silver-grey lamps → Puerto Montt & Temuco; pole paint: white w/ black number → Arica, yellow → other Atacama cities, black round → El Salvador

**Special cars & trekkers**: 'Outback' trailer behind the car → south of Santiago zoo; grey Hilux w/ snorkel → Baños Morales; white truck w/ mirrors + antenna → Conguillío NP; white pickup → **Puerto Williams** (southernmost covered town, far from all other coverage); ALMA radio telescopes (2 trekkers); horse trekker → NE of San Felipe (Argentina has a similar one!); Torres del Paine = glacier walk w/ orange helmets
All mainland coverage is **tripod** coverage — motion blur, slightly saturated colours, no car

shared_with.note: Hong Kong has full **Google-car** coverage (not tripod).
shared_with.diff: See a car/normal road coverage with Chinese signage → think HK (or Macau/Taiwan), not the mainland.

shared_with.note: Macau also has full Google-car coverage.
shared_with.diff: Car coverage + Portuguese/Chinese bilingual signs → Macau.

hints.text: **Simplified Chinese** characters (reduced stroke count)

shared_with.diff: Complex many-stroke characters + car coverage + scooters everywhere → Taiwan.

shared_with.note: Hong Kong uses **traditional** Chinese (+ much English).
shared_with.diff: Traditional characters + English + left-hand traffic → HK.

hints.text: Plates **blue with white text**
Coverage = several **small spots**, mainly in/near major east-coast urban areas; a big share is **hidden** coverage (not on Google Maps, only via Map-Making App)
Much coverage is **inside museums** — most covered museums are in/around **Beijing & Shanghai**
**Ancient towns** with coverage: Zhoucun, NE of Xuzhou, Yangzhou, west of Jiaxing, west of Zhangzhou
**Outdoor urban** coverage: Beijing, Yangzhou, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Chengdu
Plate 1st character = **province indicator** (京 Beijing, 沪 Shanghai, 粤 Guangdong…) — learnable
**Palace Museum / Forbidden City** — massive palace in the centre of Beijing
**Today Art Museum**: modern street with cafés/bars + an uncanny statue out front
**Sichuan Museum**: parking area with lots of greenery
**Hubei Museum**: palace-like architecture with grey roofs
**Museum of Silk Production**: rounded glass facade + fountain at the front
**Long Museum**: boxy concrete main building
**Suzhou Museum**: white-and-grey symmetric angular architecture
**Yangzhou Museum**: beige rounded stone building
**Zhoucun** = the only pinpointable ancient city — street runs straight north–south
Australian external territory SSW of Java (counts as Australia); car = a unique **silver pickup w/ its truck bed visible** — usually all you need to ID (don't confuse w/ Cocos Islands)
**Yellow** licence plates
Mostly dense **tropical forest**; some unpaved roads have bright **white sandy soil**; palms in places
Standard **Australian bollard** (white, red front / grey back reflector); Aussie speed signs & green sign backgrounds
Built-up areas in the **NE**; a boat trekker hugs the steep rocky north coast (white deck pokes through the blur); many trail trekkers in dense foliage
Famous for **red crabs** (trekker among hundreds near Ethel Beach; crab references on signs); harbour recognisable by large **cranes**
Two coral atolls, Australian territory (counts as Australia); **bright white sand, palms & mowed grass** — far less forested/hilly than Christmas Island
**West Island** car = unique w/ a **snorkel at the front-right** + bed edges visible; **Bantam** car = white w/ rear edges visible (may be fully blurred)
**Bantam Village** = cross-hatched brick pavement; the runway is the **widest paved road** (mowed grass both sides + a follow car)
Many **coast trekkers** on white sand (ocean one side, palms the other); **Direction Island** beaches have noticeably more litter (bricks, styrofoam, dead coral)
**Horsburgh Island** (Pulau Luar) inland trekker = dry yellow grass + sparse vegetation
Plates typically **yellow**
hints.uniq_note: Within South America yellow plates are almost exclusive to Colombia (Peru has some).

shared_with.diff: White cross-back signposts → Colombia; B&W striped posts → Peru.

hints.text: Gen3: short thick **antenna, sometimes on a black car** (also white/grey); Gen4: white or silver car
hints.uniq_note: MX/EC/BR share the antenna — but only Colombia pairs it with a BLACK car.

hints.text: Signposts of **white metal forming a distinct cross on the back** of signs
hints.uniq_note: Brazil has white posts too but wider; Costa Rica's back crosses sit on grey posts.

hints.text: km markers: tall **green 'Pr' marker** + traditional stone marker w/ black text — both Colombia-unique; bottom digits = road number + section (sections rise W→E on even roads, S→N on odd)
Poles marked w/ **black-yellow or black-orange stripes**; generally concrete, **often dark-coloured** (darker than neighbours); octagonal poles possible (Americas: only CO/MX/EC)
**Wooden fences** particularly common — painted posts w/ **sharp-cut tops** are almost exclusive to Colombia
hints.uniq_note: The painted sharp-top variant is the Colombia tell; plain wood fences are everywhere.

hints.text: Taxis **pastel yellow** w/ WHITE plates, plate often printed on side & roof (+ city name)

shared_with.note: Ecuadorian taxis = more saturated yellow + typically ORANGE plates.

hints.text: Sidewalks commonly decorated w/ **reddish tiles**
Coverage mainly **north & west** + the islands **San Andrés & Providencia**

**North** = white sandy soil, arid; **Llanos** (east of the Andes) = open tropical grassland/savanna w/ scattered trees + seasonal flooding; **sugar cane** along the Cauca river (Valle del Cauca); Pacific coast = predominantly Afro-Colombian population
Pole markings: short black strips top & bottom + **long yellow middle** → Antioquia (sometimes Córdoba)
Camera glitch making part of the image look **oversaturated** → southern Colombia

**Spotlights**: La Guajira = extreme dryness + cacti (Uribia–Puerto Bolívar road: dead straight w/ a train track always to the east); **Ruta 90 landbridge** (Barranquilla↔Ciénaga) = dashed yellow lines + shallow water both sides + ocean north
**Barranquilla**: intersections marked by small concrete **'mojones'** posts bearing the street names (e.g. Carrera 42 × Calle 68)

**Unique vehicles**: Leticia (southernmost town) = unique vehicle + long concrete blocks on the road north; **San Andrés** = distinct white car; **Providencia** = grey truck + green street lights on trapezoidal metal supports (support shape also in Barranquilla)
Gen4 = **large white pickup truck w/ a front antenna**, often fully or partly visible

shared_with.note: Panama's truck is almost always fully blurred.

hints.text: Plates short, white w/ blue text → **light-blue tint** through the blur
Lush tropical: rolling hills, agricultural lowlands, **peaky volcanoes through the centre**, dense rainforests dotted around
Less colonial than Panama: **dark-red sheet-metal/plastic-tile roofs**, pastel concrete walls, low or boxy 2-storey houses — **heavily fenced & barred**
**Spanish** official (Brazil = Portuguese)
Stop signs say **'ALTO'**

shared_with.note: Panama's ALTO uses a thicker font.

shared_with.diff: White pickup → CR; grey roof-rack car → GT.

hints.text: **Red taxis** are the standard
**Long silver insulators** on poles

shared_with.note: Panama mixes many insulator types (short silver, long black, UFO, mushroom).
Brazil can have long silver insulators.

hints.text: Common poletop: even crossbar w/ **uneven insulators (2-on-1)**, crossbar set lower than usual, usually **with support bars**; also single side-insulator at the very top; thin **L-bent metal crossbars**

shared_with.note: 2-on-1 low crossbars exist there, but usually WITHOUT support bars — and crossbars are rarer overall.

hints.text: Pole lamps w/ a **blue dot** on top (also PA/CO/BR)
Road signs often have a **cross on the back** (esp. diamond warnings)

shared_with.note: Colombian signs almost always have back crosses — but on WHITE signposts.
shared_with.diff: Grey posts → CR; white posts → CO.

hints.text: 'Bollard' = vertical **orange board w/ 3 black circles**
Electricity counters **circular, often w/ a big metal box** beside them (circular also in Mexico etc.)
**Imperial beer ads** w/ the black eagle mascot everywhere
**Provinces** appear on signs + have their own flags; area codes moderately useful
**Pole-marking metas**:
bullets: Large black/white pole stickers: first digit of the top number = province
Asymmetric poletops (1+2 insulators): in/around San José (fully one-sided: countrywide)
Wire-formed numbers on poles: central Alajuela + Heredia
White pole plates w/ black letters (metal bands): Greater Metropolitan Area
Blue 'P-XXXXX' code: western Cartago
QR-code stickers: around Corralillo, south of San José
Yellow paint, tall w/ 3 numbered sections: Puntarenas + S-Alajuela
Yellow paint, short, no dividers: Guanacaste + NW-Alajuela
Yellow paint, 2 sections + horizontal characters below: almost only S-Alajuela
Black sticker w/ yellow letters near pole base: W-Guanacaste + Nicoya

hints.text: **Landscape & crops**:
bullets: Caribbean Lowlands (north): flat farmland, grey dirt, straight roads, often overcast
West: coverage mostly from the dry season
Bananas: Limón · Oil palms: coastal Puntarenas (+ some Limón)
Pineapple: W-Limón, N-Alajuela, Buenos Aires · Sugarcane: several areas (blooming silvery tufts → San Isidro/Buenos Aires)
Rice paddies: south of Bagaces (Guanacaste) · Coffee: central valley · Cassava: W-Limón
Teak (straight trunks, glossy round leaves): Guanacaste
Guanacaste trees (huge umbrella crowns): Guanacaste
Attalea palms: southern coast · Macaw palms (spiky trunk, messy round crown): west
Dark volcanic rocks: central CR, esp. Guapiles + Miravalles

hints.text: Taxis carry **yellow triangles w/ town name + province letter code**; driftwood-branch fences → west; irrigation canals → south of Bagaces/Cañas; stilt houses → NE coast; folded-down car antenna → north/NE
**San José**: biggest urban area, valley w/ inclined roads, taller buildings; municipality-wide **green street signs w/ white emblem rectangle** — Calle/Avenida numbers increase outward from downtown

**Road spotlights**: few train tracks (GMA → Puntarenas west; via Turrialba → Siquirres/Limón east); only concrete highways = eastern RN 18 + divided RN 1 Liberia↔RN 18; RN 32 (RN 4→Limón) = divided through flat lush lowlands, w/ **yellow centre bollards** between toll & RN 4; yellow follow-car w/ 2 front windows → RN 2 east of Palmar Norte; wind turbines → Guanacaste clusters + SW of Cartago; bright-white soil roads → north of Liberia; night coverage → San Vito; tuk-tuks → Puerto Viejo de Talamanca (+ Nosara)

**Rainbolt coverage**: Rainbolt / film crew on a black Toyota around **La Fortuna**; his Gen4 trekker (brown hair, camera crew, stair trail → waterfall) SW of La Fortuna; another tropical-trail trekker (black-haired) in Mistico Park west of La Fortuna

**Gen3 = trekkers only**: around San José + NW coast beaches, Cocos Island (500 km offshore) + Caño Island boat. Pinpointable ones incl. Children's Museum (castle look), National Museum tower, orange bridge at Parque de la Paz, La Sabana/National Stadium, University of CR, Palacio de los Deportes (Heredia), airport walk, 3 dogs in pine forest (Bosque de la Hoja), Cartago museum/ruins/stadium, Punta Leona resort (Turkish-style red bricks), Liberia white building, Filadelfia L-shaped bricks
Plates white — often **fully white in Gen3** (strip commoner on newer/coastal cars); **HR stickers** on older cars
Bollards white w/ black rectangle + red/white reflector, **triangular from above (sharp edges)**

shared_with.note: Sometimes similar — but MK is Gen3-only.

hints.text: **Croatian**: Latin-only Slavic; **'IJE'** where Serbian uses 'E'; Č Ć Đ Š Ž

shared_with.note: Slovene has lots of J's too.

hints.text: Chevrons **red arrow on yellow or white**; direction signs **yellow w/ black outline + pencil tip** (all ex-Yugoslav countries similar)
**B-type guardrails** (rare in Europe)

Gen3: **side mirrors partially visible** (commonest here) — sometimes a **yellow circular sticker on the mirror = Croatia-unique**; most Gen3 = **autumn colours** (not Gen4); Gen4 black or white car, never w/ antenna; Gen2 = only downtown **trekkers** (red cap sometimes visible)
hints.uniq_note: The yellow mirror sticker is unique to Croatia; visible mirrors alone also appear elsewhere.

hints.text: Cylindrical concrete/wooden poles, wooden often w/ **alternating hooks** (≈ Latvia); **blue fire hydrants** w/ two diagonal protrusions (similar in Hungary)
Exposed **red-brick walls** inland (also SI/RS); pedestrian sign 5 stripes + belt (Slovenia's man: no belt)
**Karlovačko & Ožujsko** beer ads; supermarkets Konzum (+Bosnia) & **Plodine (Croatia-only)**
hints.uniq_note: Plodine exists only in Croatia — Konzum also in Bosnia (uncovered).

hints.text: **Area codes**: 0 + code; town names ending **-ec** → northern central Croatia

**NE (Slavonia)** = flat farmland (≈ Hungary/NW-Serbia); N+W of Zagreb = hilly agriculture; **Adriatic coast** = rocky mountains + shrubs + islands (≈ ME/AL); coast houses = beige brick + green shutters (Italy-like); **Istria** = red soil + Italian bilingual signs (west)
**Zagreb**: big-city feel, hilly north, **blue trams** + Gen2 sidewalk trekkers; trams only in Zagreb & Osijek; **Rijeka**: long blue traffic-light poles over the road (exclusive)

**Spotlights**: NE of Zadar = flat + distant ridge north; **Ilok** = vineyard country near the Serbian border; **Petrinja** = earthquake ruins (2020); **Dubrovnik** = medieval city walls + tall cypresses near the southern tip
Constituent country of the **Kingdom of the Netherlands**, north of Venezuela — **counts as Netherlands** in streaks
**Square plates**, looking completely white through the blur (front plates mandatory but a minority go without)
**Black car w/ a roof rack** — unique to Curaçao
Semi-arid climate → **cacti** everywhere
Simple **one-storey pastel bungalows** (metal roofs on shacks, tiles on nicer houses, some multi-storey villas)
Sign borders (red/blue) **continue onto the back of the sign**; blue street signs on **yellow signposts**
**Black-white striped traffic-light posts** in urban areas
Wooden poles w/ **3 horizontal alternating insulators**

shared_with.diff: Caribbean cacti island vs Brazil — context obvious.

hints.text: **Western half**: semi-arid rural, green-but-dry hills, spread-out houses, narrow roads

**Spotlights**: SE wind farm w/ notably **rainy coverage**; **Queen Juliana Bridge** (Willemstad) = wide 4-lane bridge w/ yellow-blue guardrails + city view; Grote Berg (centre) = a **stick stuck to the camera lens**
Plates white EU **or** UK-style **yellow rear**, mostly w/ EU strip; **red rental plates** common
Drives on the **left** (ex-UK colony — UK-style signs & road lines too)

shared_with.note: Malta also drives left with UK heritage.
shared_with.diff: Maltese/English + globigerina limestone → Malta; Greek script → Cyprus.

shared_with.note: The infrastructure itself is borrowed from the UK.

hints.text: **Greek + English** (Greek alphabet, soft lowercase shapes)

shared_with.diff: Cyprus drives LEFT + English co-signage; Greece drives right.

hints.text: Mostly Gen4 **smallcam** (low mount, big blur, front protrusion) — sometimes a **short antenna** visible at the back

shared_with.note: Greece has much smallcam too — but **never a visible antenna**.
