hints.text: Bollards like Turkish ones (red front / white back reflector) but **triangular with a slanted top**, not flat

shared_with.diff: Turkish ones are **flat**; Cypriot triangular + slanted.

hints.text: Directional signs ≈ Greek ones — but Greek text is NOT all-caps (Greece: all-caps); **town-entry signs** (yellow w/ red border or white, bilingual + speed limit above) are unique to Cyprus
Tall **dark-brown wooden poles** (like Greece); poletops: crossbar w/ 0/3 insulators, 5 side insulators, or harp-shaped frame — the **symmetric 3-insulator top is Cyprus-specific**

shared_with.diff: The symmetric 3-insulator top → Cyprus; driving side otherwise.

hints.text: Brightly painted **stone buildings**, tiled or flat roofs (Greek-like); houses often carry a **white horizontal cylindrical water tank** w/ circular lid
Mediterranean: bright exposed rock, olive/palm/cypress trees, green coastal farmland, pine-forest mountains inland

Only the **southern half** covered (Republic + Akrotiri & Dhekelia); road numbers A/B/E/F grouped by first digit (E/F near matching B road); 5 provinces = 2-digit area codes (2x); postal codes 4-digit on street signs (T.K./T.T.)
**Terrain = compass**: centre = highest; white limestone → south coast; reddish soil → interior + NW; vineyards → SW; wild mustard (yellow flowers) → mostly east; dark red fields → north + Famagusta
**Paphos Forest**: windy mountain roads, red soil, uninhabited — and (almost) no bollards; **NE**: Kyrenia ridge visible north over flat farmland; NE also has **black-top bollards** (Slovenia-like)
**Famagusta** (far east): flat + red fields — and ALL coverage is **shitcam** (rare elsewhere on the island)
**Wind turbines** in 2 clusters: west of Larnaca (sandy rolling farm hills) + SW of Anogyra (Oreites Forest, incline toward the visible coast)

**Spotlights**: snow coverage near Panagia Machaira monastery; dashed **blue lines** between road markings around Mount Olympus; banana plantations around Peyia (west coast); **Limassol** = Russian on many commercial signs (17% Russian-speaking)
Bollards: **orange double reflectors front / single white back** (rare blue/red variants)

shared_with.note: The ONLY other country with this bollard.
shared_with.diff: Czech letters Ř Ě Ů vs Slovak Ľ Ô Ä; sign borders separated from the edge → Czechia.

hints.text: **Extra-wide B-type guardrails** — only CZ+SK in Europe (SK occasionally uses others; CZ consistent)

Wide round concrete poles, often **in pairs**; poletops w/ short metal bars; upside-down-triangle supports (also DE/AL/RS/RO); **upward-angled trident tops = CZ+SK only**; lamp posts w/ thick bolted base

shared_with.diff: Street signs: red (CZ) vs white w/ red border + blue text (SK).

hints.text: Temporary square posts in **red-white stripes**; chevrons white w/ red arrows (rarer red-on-yellow); stripy **gas/water poles** roadside; **hiking markers** (colour strip between two white) — most common in CZ+SK
Prefab panel blocks in **good condition w/ vibrant colours/patterns** (nicer than elsewhere in the ex-East-bloc); **diamond-tile roofs** (also HU)
Gen3 antenna w/ **grey tape on top** (also HU/RO/BG); Gen4 red, black or **white** car (white = CZ, not SK); much **grey winter Gen3**; rare Gen2 (SK: none)

shared_with.diff: **White Gen4 car, winter Gen3 or Gen2 → Czechia** (Slovakia has none of the three).
Romanian ă î ș ț → Romania.

hints.text: Black **bridge-end signs** w/ road number (Gen4)
**Czech: Ř Ě Ů** (Slovak instead: Ľ Ô Ä Ŕ Ĺ); both use acute vowels (á é í …) — South Slavic doesn't; street = **ulice** (most Slavic: ulica)
Rolling hills, constant altitude changes, few tall mountains (SK: more mountains AND more true flats); solid outer lines w/o centre line common (also DE)

**CZ-vs-SK sign kit**: directional signs arrow-shaped w/ filled tip (SK: small white arrows); sign borders **separated from the edge** (SK: on the edge); highway signs w/ double border; street signs **bright red** (or blue; SK: white w/ red border + blue text); house numbers blue/red w/ white borders (SK: white w/ red/black border); posts w/ **thick black caps** (SK: none); ped signs 5 full-size stripes (SK smaller-scale); **thin sign font** (SK bold); km markers w/ yellow outline, no middle line (SK: middle line, no yellow); railway-cross signs w/o background (SK: white rectangle); bike signs yellow
Suburban houses commonly **connected in pairs** (rarer in all neighbours)

**Road numbers**: first digit 1–5 regional (6 scattered; 7/8/9 = Prague northward); 4-digit **bike paths increase clockwise** from S-Bohemia (A-paths = Prague); area codes = first 1–3 digits (SK numbers start with 0)
**Moravia** = 95% of Czech wine; N-Bohemia = rolling land + steep solitary hills (sometimes castle-topped)

**Architecture regions**: timber frames → northern German border; thick dark wooden support poles → far north; dark wooden houses w/ white line stripes → north; Moravian townhouses = long side to the road + **barn-sized doors** (also NE-Austria); **folk baroque** (decorated walls) → around České Budějovice; single-pane windows → northwest

**Street-sign colours = region**: blue → east (Ostrava = only major blue city); white w/ blue text + red border (SK-style) → Slovak border; white w/ red text → east + far south (**Brno**); white w/ blue text → east + central north; white w/ black text → southeast

**City metas**: Prague = grey-white marble mosaic pavement (Portugal-like — check city size!); Zlín = functionalist red-brick Baťa cubes; Brno = **black+white house-number pairs** (district on the black one); Pilsen = two red-white octagonal plates; České Budějovice = red+white underlined plates; **Liberec** = red numbers w/ dividing line + Roman numeral; Písek = yellow numbers w/ black outline; Šumperk = blue signs, text fills the sign
**Polish bilingual street signs** → Český Těšín (rarely nearby)
Plates long white + blue strip; commercial **yellow**; **'Parrot' plates** (yellow left section) unique to Denmark
hints.uniq_note: The half-yellow parrot plate exists nowhere else — plain yellow plates also in NL/LU/UK.

hints.text: Generally **flat**, wide open fields; airy suburbs, dense urban cores
All-white lines; **double middles** common; outer lines of **small white squares** — a great Denmark tell
**Pipe-framed directional signs low to the ground** (red-white or blue-white, arrow in the background) + the pipe-frame signpost bent around signs — unique
Street names end **-vej**; street signs blue w/ white text
White bollard w/ **yellow reflector + dark-orange stripe** (green variant exists)
hints.uniq_note: Unique to Denmark — Belgian lookalikes never have the orange stripe.

hints.text: **Danish: Æ Ø Å** (Norwegian identical; Faroese Æ+Ø; Icelandic Æ only; Swedish Å+ÄÖ)
**Red brick** everywhere (orange/brown/black tile roofs); thatched roofs rural; grey corrugated (Baltic-like); timber framing on old buildings
**B-type guardrails without reflectors** (rare in Europe); street-level poles rare — mostly big overhead power lines near highways
Pedestrian sign **5 stripes — first & fifth touch the triangle edge** (unique detail); town-entry sign = white silhouette sign, exit = red slash; chevrons red-white, low to the ground
Bus stops **black-yellow** w/ schedule below (older blue-white version exists)
Bicycle paths (also NL/BE); **PostNord red postal boxes**; gas stations: Ingo (also SE), Ok (also NL), Uno-X/Yx (also NO); rare stone bollards w/ red tops near bike paths

**Road numbers ordered east→west**; 2–5 grouped by region, 1x nationwide (2nd digit ≈ region; Bornholm only 1x); **ring roads O1–O4** around Copenhagen, Odense, Aarhus, Aalborg, Hillerød, Kolding, Vejle
**Six regional bus companies** (brand on the front + colour scheme) + region-specific bus-stop signs
**Greenland & Faroe Islands** belong to Denmark — separate pages here
**Bornholm**: green-red-white bus stops + green bicycle signs; **Læsø**: traditional **seaweed (eelgrass) roofs**; **Anholt**: only gloomy overcast Gen2 + sandy roadsides
Unique car on ALL coverage: **two metal bars w/ thick black stripes** + white hood visible at the front
hints.uniq_note: One Mongolian car has similar stripes (landscape separates); Guatemala/Curaçao have metal bars WITHOUT thick black stripes.

shared_with.note: A Mongolian car has similar striped bars.
Metal bars, but no thick black stripes; mirrors visible.

hints.text: Poles mostly **square concrete w/ a reverse-'L' top** (round/octagonal rarer)

shared_with.note: The only other square-pole country in the Americas.

hints.text: Plates **pale yellow top / white bottom** (New-Jersey-like); front plates not required (in LatAm otherwise only PR + Panama)
Roads often of **smooth, noticeably darker asphalt**
Stop signs say **'PARE'** despite the Central American location; many signs on **yellow signposts**
Coverage very limited: **only Santo Domingo + Santiago de los Caballeros**

**Mountains to the north = always Santiago de los Caballeros** (Santo Domingo has hills, never real mountains); Santiago also has **yellow traffic-light poles** (yellow signposts exist in both cities!)

**Santo Domingo**: developed downtown w/ tall buildings + wide avenues; street signs w/ **'ADN'** (Distrito Nacional); **black-purple pole markings** only here; outskirts = whitish dirt tracks, dry shrubs, debris
Some **late-evening coverage in Santo Domingo** — Santiago has none
Plates white, a **mix of short & long** — the only Latin American country with both; commercial (taxis/buses) = **solid orange** or orange top bar
hints.uniq_note: The short+long mix is the tell; orange commercial plates also exist in Chile.

hints.text: Gen3: white car w/ **stubby coiled antenna**; Gen4: **white pickup w/ black trunk, NO antenna**

shared_with.note: Same antenna; on a BLACK car it's Colombia-only (Colombia can have white too).
PA/CR pickups have a FRONT antenna.
shared_with.diff: No antenna on the truck → Ecuador.

shared_with.note: CR truck has an antenna and is often visible.

hints.text: **Shitcam** coverage (official Ari): wide circular blur, brownish colours, sometimes a **red logo** at the front — the only Latin American country with it
**Bollards very common** (rare in LatAm): round w/ **2 red stripes**, or flat black w/ 2 red reflectors (variants keep the scheme)
hints.uniq_note: Within Latin America a bollard-heavy round leans Ecuador; a similar rectangle exists in Malaysia.

hints.text: Poles mostly round concrete (generic); the useful one: **ladder pole w/ MANY small indents** — more or less unique in LatAm (Brazil's waffle poles have only a few)

shared_with.note: Brazilian ladder/waffle poles have far fewer indents.

hints.text: **Concrete roads** relatively common; **double guardrails** very common (rare elsewhere in S. America)
**Chevron spam** in curves — far more chevrons than most of LatAm (Colombia occasionally too); lots of **square white road signs**
km markers **green w/ the Ecuadorian highway shield**
Sign backs sometimes painted **teal blue** (thin metal posts) — the only LatAm country; one/two-way signs w/ **text inside the arrow** (otherwise only DR & PR in LatAm)
hints.uniq_note: Teal backs = instant Ecuador within South America.

hints.text: Houses mostly **grey brick + metal roofs**; hexagonal-tile pavement in some urban areas

shared_with.note: Bolivia is dominated by red brick.

hints.text: Coverage centred **west** (much of the Amazon uncovered; Galápagos = trekkers); Gen4 nearly everywhere except NW + SE; **antenna meta: 'high antenna' (wire above halfway) = always northern half**

**Landscape**: Andes through the middle, flat east & west; bananas → southwest; extremely dry coast → only Guayaquil↔Manta; flattest → around Guayaquil (E-25/E-48); **Amazon** = flat + white-barked Cordia trees + oil palms (almost always high antenna); Esmeraldas = Amazon-like but hillier; south = winding mountain roads w/ exposed brown-red dirt; Chimborazo = semi-arid + pines

**Taxi/plate letter = province** (first letter usually matches the province name; beware P = Pichincha/Quito appears elsewhere); poles w/ oval see-through holes → Loja; black-yellow painted guardrails → Guayas

**Spotlights**: E-40 (Salinas↔Guayaquil) = one of the only divided highways, in the dry SW; brown pipe alongside some NE roads; **Galápagos** = trekkers + boats only — volcanic soil, low shrubs, craters, giant tortoises
Very sparse — **all Gen3 trekker** coverage (low camera, small blurs, no car shadow) at **7 historic sites** in Cairo & Alexandria
Most buildings are **sandstone**; Arabic script (lines + dots) occasionally on signs
**Giza pyramids** trekker (don't confuse with Memphis's glass pyramid)
**Khafre valley temple** = rows of massive square pillars; **Djoser step pyramid** (stairs + scaffolding) south of Cairo
**Cairo Citadel**: huge clean-cut sandstone walls + Ottoman **Mosque of Muhammad Ali** (grey domes, tall minarets)
**Hanging Church** (Cairo, near Fustat): urban street + church spires w/ crosses, then interior courtyard w/ mosaics & two white spires
**Alexandria — Qaitbay Citadel**: sandstone castle surrounded by thick walls
**Saint Mina monastery** (palace-like, Christian spires/crosses) + **Abu Mena** excavation (small yellow wooden chapel) SW of Alexandria
Plates long white + EU strip
Bollards **round, baton-like**: rectangular front reflector + two circular back reflectors (white or orange)
Least consistent Baltic poles: round wooden (± diagonal supports) + square concrete (LT-like); trident tops; wooden poles w/ **Nordic metal caps** (also SE/FI/NO, sometimes LV)
Outer lines sometimes **short dashes** (also LT; Sweden mostly)

Chevrons **red w/ white arrows** (like RU/UA; LV/LT inverted!); Baltic km markers: Estonia's **perpendicular to the road, numbers both sides**; warning-sign border thin red **to the edge** (no white outline); **guardrails without reflectors** (LV: white/red, LT: orange)
Pedestrian signs 3 stripes + **detailed person (shoes, attached head)** (LT: simpler, LV: 5 stripes); bus-stop sign 4-window bus (LV: 5); **blue-white signpost wrappings** (also FI/SE)
**Estonian** (Finnic!): Ä Ö Ü + **Õ**; street = **tänav (tn)** / road = **tee** — unique words (Finnish -tie looks similar); many streets lack a suffix entirely
**Oval blue house numbers** (not LV/LT; also Russia)
Wood + white brick, steep corrugated metal roofs; **most wooden buildings of the Baltics**
Directional signs blue/white w/ white outline (FI builds the arrow into the border; LV arrows more stylised)
Gen3 often has a **'green blur'** tint on part of the panorama (not LV/LT; also Gotland/Sweden); Gen3 covers many **very rural roads** — thin rural Gen3 in a Baltic round leans Estonia
hints.uniq_note: The green blur is the Estonia tell within the Baltics — Sweden's Gotland shares it, but the language separates instantly.

hints.text: Fairly flat (max 318 m) — hilliest: southeast + southern Lääne-Viru; **Russian bilingual signs** → Ida-Viru (Narva area)
Old town = perfectly preserved **medieval/renaissance** area (Central-Europe look) — unlike anywhere else in Estonia
Island: all **Gen4 late-autumn** (brown leaves) + moody dark near-sunset clouds (Tartu has some autumn too)
Landscape = **green wooded hills + tall golden grass** (Lesotho's mountains are barren, w/ little long grass)
Poles dark brown wood, insulators mostly side-mounted — often **3 insulators** (commonest in Eswatini within S. Africa)
Roads starting with **"MR"** are exclusive to Eswatini
**Yellow outer lines**, occasionally triple white middle lines (shared with ZA/Lesotho/Botswana)
**White car, no antenna** (also ZA / Lesotho / Botswana)
Plates **long, green at the bottom** (resemble some South African plates); concrete bus shelters common
West = montane golden grass + pine plantations; MR9 central-south = dark-green wooded mountains; east = flat + sugarcane; Portuguese → Lomahasha (Mozambique border)

British Overseas Territory east of Tierra del Fuego — **counts as UK** in streaks; coverage = only a few **Gen3 trekkers** on 3 western islands (**Carcass, West Point, New Island**), none on streets → memorise the trekkers, not metas
**Carcass Island** trekker: open plains, ocean clearly visible, sometimes rocky ground
hints.uniq_note: Don't confuse it with the Faroe Islands trekkers — those have mountains rising from the sea.

shared_with.diff: Jagged fjord mountains → Faroes; low open plains → Falklands.

hints.text: **West Point Island**: open plains w/ slightly yellow grass, ocean NOT clearly visible; boat trekker in West Point Bay w/ the **MS National Geographic Explorer** + yellow hills
**New Island**: sandy beach w/ people in red & blue jackets; inland = dark brown soil + tyre tracks; **distinct steep cliffs**
Long wide **fjords + jagged green mountains rising straight from the water**, rocky ridgelines along the mountainsides; **almost no naturally growing trees**

shared_with.note: Icelandic coastal mountains look similar but less imposing and less green.
shared_with.diff: Greener + more imposing → Faroes; volcanic black gravel → Iceland.

hints.text: Gen4 = **black pickup truck w/ a front antenna** (always blurred); Gen3 = **black car w/ a plain grey roof rack**
hints.uniq_note: No other country with a similar landscape has these cars — Iceland's truck lacks the antenna.

shared_with.note: Iceland's Gen4 truck has NO front antenna (and is red & white).

hints.text: Houses of **wood on an above-ground stone basement** painted a different colour; roofs **metal or turf (grass)**
hints.uniq_note: The two-tone wood-on-stone build with turf roofs is the classic Faroese look.

hints.text: **Faroese** (closest to Icelandic): Á Ð Í Ó Ú Ý Æ + **Ø**

shared_with.note: Icelandic shares most letters but uses É and Þ instead of Ø.

shared_with.diff: Ð/accented vowels → Faroese; flat Denmark vs fjord walls.

hints.text: Bollards = small **wooden sticks painted yellow**, sometimes red on top
Guardrails w/ **yellow reflectors** — no other northern European country has them
hints.uniq_note: Within northern Europe unique; Andorra/Spain/Belgium use yellow too but look nothing alike.

hints.text: Directional signs **yellow w/ thin black border + metal frame**

shared_with.note: Very similar yellow signs in Iceland.
shared_with.diff: Ø + turf roofs → Faroes; ð/þ → Iceland.

hints.text: **Tórshavn**: by far the biggest town, the only place w/ buildings **taller than 3 floors**; relatively open landscape, esp. to the east
**Klaksvík** (2nd biggest): narrow **N-S fjord** w/ fairly steep hills
**Wind parks** (3 visible): largest NW of Tórshavn; others NE of Tórshavn + east of Vestmanna; **Kirkjubøur** (southern tip): black houses w/ grass roofs + small church ruin
**Trekkers**:
bullets: Koltur island: narrow low peninsula on the SE tip
Eiðiskollur: extremely low clouds around the peninsula
West of Skopun: wide strait north w/ the main islands behind
Klakkur: two pyramid-shaped mountains south w/ a thin snow layer
Gjógv: village view south w/ snowy mountains behind
Grey boat 'RIB62' w/ people in reflective clothes → en route to Koltur

hints.text: **Shorter-than-normal** white plates + EU strip
Outer lines **always solid white** (SE/NO use dashes — exception: Åland!); middle lines: white dashes ± solid white, solid yellow + white dashes, or double yellow (yellow being phased out)

shared_with.note: Sweden never has yellow middles; outers dashed on small roads.
Norwegian middles have an orange tint + dashed outers.

hints.text: Long b&w bollards: **rectangular white front reflector + two dots on the back**, diagonal black strip (round or thin-curved)
Snow poles orange w/ **thin white band near the TOP**
**Finnish**: unrelated to most of Europe — long words, double vowels, ä/ö

shared_with.note: Estonian is closely related and looks similar.

hints.text: Street names end **-tie / -katu** (also -polku, -kuja); street signs white w/ black text
Road signs **yellow background** + red-border signs w/ a **thin yellow stroke on the edge** — no other Nordic country has that stroke
hints.uniq_note: Sweden/Iceland also have yellow signs — the thin yellow edge stroke is the Finland-only detail.

hints.text: Pedestrian signs **5 stripes** (SE/NO usually 4); directional signs solid blue w/ white border-arrows; chevrons **black w/ yellow arrows** (NO/IS similar; SE = blue-yellow)
Wooden poles; lamps mounted **at the top w/ straight arm + double bolting** (SE: lower + curved); **blue-white striped signposts** (SE too; Estonian ones wider/looser)
Dense **boreal forest** (pines + white-trunk birches); Gen3 often in **autumn** (rare in other Nordics — fall vibe = Finland tell); **lakes everywhere** ('Land of a Thousand Lakes')
Modern utilitarian architecture; wooden cladding; **Falu-red** country houses (like SE/NO)
Only **two bus-stop styles**: blue-white or yellow-black (Sweden has many, mostly with stop names)
**K-Market & S-Market** supermarkets exist only in Finland
**Shitcam** coverage (most in Europe together w/ Spain); Gen4 smallcam
**Regional 3–4-digit road numbers: first digit = region**, 1 (south) → 9 (north); 2-digit majors don't follow

**Region metas**: Lapland big roads (2023+) = snow poles w/ 2 long reflectors; farmland → west + south coast; wind turbines → west coast; stunted spindly trees → north; **Gen3 snow only in far-NW Lapland**
**Swedish bilingual** → south + west coasts (Swedish inland ≈ never); **Sámi bilingual** (uses the letter **c**) → far north
Gen3 antenna metas: slightly **tilted long antenna** → southwest; **no antenna** → south-central

**Åland**: monolingual Swedish; red-tinted pavement + **dashed outer lines** (Sweden-like); short fully-white plates w/ blue text, no EU strip; some Swedish 4-stripe ped signs; bus stops w/ white 4-digit plate (+semicircle sign); **no Gen4**
**Road 970** (Norwegian border): river valley, river usually NW, stunted birches, dead autumn Gen3; **Kilpisjärvi** = hilly low-vegetation Scandes foothills (spring Gen2, dead look)

**Helsinki**: much Gen4 sidewalk trekker downtown; **green-yellow trams** (Tampere's are red); buses blue-white w/ HSL logo; **Tampere** buses 'NYSSE'; Turku = yellow buses w/ white rear; Jyväskylä = green buses; Oulu = pink-front buses

**Tampere**: red metal plates w/ 7 golden protrusions (houses/lamps/posts); Seinäjoki & Isokyrö = unique blue street signs; **Lahti** = black-border ALL-CAPS street signs (Kuopio thicker font); Kerava = blue-yellow coat of arms top-left; Järvenpää = blue-grey arms + neighbourhood above the name
Plates long white, **blue strips both sides** (left more visible; rare old plates: yellow rear)

shared_with.note: Italian plates have two **equally visible** blue strips.
shared_with.diff: Italian front plates are much **shorter**; language decides.

hints.text: **French**: é à è ù â ê î ô û ë ï ç œ — the **-ux suffix** is French-only; street = **rue**

Iconic bollard: round white, **pointed top, reflector band all around** (grey or red); variants: two indented bands (also green/yellow) or wedge-shaped w/ wide rectangular reflectors — both France-only
hints.uniq_note: The two rarer designs are unique to France; the classic pointed bollard has a Scottish lookalike with a **blunt** top — pointed = France.

hints.text: **Ladder/waffle poles** (step-indent concrete) + 'French poletops' (diamond, 3 wires)

shared_with.note: Ladder poles + French poletops are common in Spain too.
shared_with.diff: Blue pole plates → France; Spanish signage → Spain.

hints.text: Rural: wooden + thin octagonal metal poles; small **blue rectangular plates ('stickers') on poles** = unique to France; poletop w/ **insulators on top** (arrowhead look) = unique
Gen4 **smallcam** (low mount, big blur, front protrusion) — like most of Western Europe
Small **yellow road-number signs** ('D…') on top of directional/town signs = unique; directional signs white w/ offset black border, arrow-shaped; highways blue/green; italic font for POIs

Town entry + road signs: **red border w/ thin white outline** (Spain: thick border to the edge); white sub-signs w/ black text; **flat rectangular signposts** (elsewhere only PT/ES/AD) often very short; unorthodox sign colours common

shared_with.diff: Spain's red sign border reaches the edge; Spanish language.

hints.text: Chevrons **white on blue**, 1–5 arrows (Spain: only 2 or 4); pedestrian sign: 5 stripes + **disconnected head** (or dashed-line variant)

shared_with.note: Same blue chevrons — but only with 2 or 4 arrows.

hints.text: Km markers: white w/ **yellow or red semicircle top** (waystone or flat, parallel or perpendicular); highways: square w/ red top
Outer lines = long spaced dashes; **'Z-block' middle dashes** (two offset blocks) = France-only; unusually long middle dashes (also UK); end-of-passing-zone arrows (also ES/PT/RO; UK's point left)
hints.uniq_note: The offset-block middle line is unique — the rest of the line metas are shared (UK/ES/PT/RO).

hints.text: **A-type guardrails without reflectors** (Spain/Andorra/Belgium use yellow reflectors; rare thin B-type); roadside **Austrian-flag-coloured flag markers** found nowhere else
hints.uniq_note: The red-white-red flag markers are unique; plain A-type guardrails are pan-European.

hints.text: Stone houses, unpainted/grey/yellow pastel; simple gable roofs w/ orange tiles or black slate; wall-to-wall townhouses (very diverse regional styles!); **blue serif house numbers** (Italy almost never blue)
**Peugeot/Renault/Citroën** ≈ half of all cars

**13 regions / 96 departments** (on the map!); area codes: 01 Île-de-France, 02–05 clockwise from NW; secondary 2X north → anticlockwise (9X = far NW + far SE); **postal codes: first 2 digits = department number** (mostly alphabetical — same code in the right plate strip)
**Department logos** on bus stops, km markers, signbacks (Occitanie = red-yellow Occitan cross; Pyrénées-Orientales = Senyera stripes — Provence similar but vertical)
**Red/burgundy-tinted roads** → between Paris and Dijon (+Normandy) — historical Burgundy

**Coloured sign infra**: dark green posts/backs → Brittany, Paris area, south; Eure-et-Loir = green logo sticker + superscript **D-road decimals** (D134.10); grey-green back + darker post → Côtes-d'Armor/E-Ille-et-Vilaine, Pyrénées-Atlantiques, Alps; Pas-de-Calais = dark green post + lighter green back (+triangle logo); brown post+back → Seine-et-Marne; Haute-Garonne = burgundy attachments + yellow posts; Marne = combined thick town entry/exit blocks

**Topography**: tallest mountains SE + Spanish border; north/west flat-rolling, south hillier; NW (Normandy) = hedge-lined UK-like roads; stone pines → south coast (to Nantes); flat **maritime-pine forests** → Gironde/Landes; vineyards → Bordeaux, south coast, Champagne, Loire, Alsace, Corsica; sunflowers → SW (Toulouse); wind turbines → north

**Architecture regions**: black shingle roofs → NW of the Loire (+Belgian border, south-centre, Pyrenees — good Alps-vs-Pyrenees tell); Brittany = black roofs + edge chimneys + slate walls + stone-framed windows; rounded south-tiles; dark-red Flemish brick → Belgian border; bright orange brick → Toulouse; cream/orange stone w/ brick details → Paris-area towns; Riviera = Monaco-like palms & balconies (+harbour boat trekkers); Basque = white houses w/ coloured timber (Spanish Basque = unpainted); Alpine wood houses; Alsace = German timber framing + fish-scale roofs

**Minority languages**: Breton (Celtic, k/w/ñ, street = straed; Plou- towns → NW Brittany); Alsatian (gasse/strasse); Occitan (carrièra Toulouse / carriero Avignon); Basque (K X Z, street = karrika, thin font) → W-Pyrénées-Atlantiques; Corsican (Italian-like, -u endings); Catalan (carrer, ç) → Pyrénées-Orientales — French always dominates!
**Toponym suffix map** + rivers: -sur-[river] (shortened ˢ/), -sur-mer/-plage = coast only
**Overseas** (count as France): Réunion, Saint-Pierre & Miquelon, Martinique — each has its own page here

**Paris**: mansard roofs (steep black shingle + dormers) on wide boulevards; **Corsica**: stone-mortar cuboid barriers (rounded-top ones ≠ Corsica); **Belle-Île-en-Mer**: Brittany houses w/ colourful facades, only oversaturated **Gen2**; lavender fields → SE; salt farms → Atlantic coast (Guérande, Île de Ré...)

**Street-sign city metas**: Paris/Lyon/Marseille = the only cities w/ **arrondissements** (on the map!); Paris = green & Prussian-blue arch design (imitated in Bucharest!); Lyon = 4 corner bolts + notched inner border; Marseille = Greek-cross emblem + top-interrupted border; Bordeaux = 'Arrᵗ' tables (despite no arrondissements) + bordeaux-red posts in the outskirts; Montpellier = blue 'M' top-left; **Lille = red slanted fleur-de-lis** (unique)

**Riviera**: azure-blue painted signposts (Cagnes-sur-mer = powder blue); thin **B-type guardrails** on some northern highways (Le Mans/Tours/Paris/Reims/Metz axes); Gen3 **antenna flags** (colourful, France-unique) — distribution learnable
All car coverage = **Gen4** (only some Gen2/3 trekkers + spill)
B&W bollards w/ **white/light-grey reflectors** (orange at intersections); often road-number plates + **blue side attachments**; a bollard roughly **every 50 m**

shared_with.note: Almost identical bollards — but with **3 bolts** on the reflector (Germany: 2).
shared_with.diff: Count the bolts; Luxembourgish/French signage → Luxembourg.

hints.text: **German**: Ä Ö Ü **ß**

shared_with.diff: ß on signs → Germany/Austria; Alpine landscape + low-cam → Switzerland.
EINBAHN (vs Einbahnstraße), different town signs → Austria.
FL plates, tiny alpine country → Liechtenstein.

hints.text: Wooden poles w/ **rectangular white sticker**; many poletop styles: uneven hanging insulators, triangle tops, T-shaped tops
**Black/dark-grey sign backs** (rare in Europe) + sticker with a red upside-down triangle on black
Bus stops with the **yellow-green H symbol** (regional designs, like Austria); mailboxes **bright yellow** w/ Deutsche-Post horn logo
**Yellow town-entry signs** (black text, metal frame): town on top, municipality middle, county bottom
Km markers: **blue w/ white border = Autobahn**; white w/ black border = B/local roads; triangular markers on smaller roads (regional)
Pedestrian signs: **5 stripes + person with belt** (same in Luxembourg); **Einbahnstraße** one-way signs unique (Austria writes EINBAHN); no-stopping signs w/ white arrows
hints.uniq_note: The word Einbahnstraße is Germany-only (Austria uses EINBAHN); the belt pedestrian sign is shared with Luxembourg.

hints.text: Cars: **green circular windshield sticker** (number 4; rarely yellow 3 / red 2) bottom-right on nearly every car
**Wind turbines** far more common than in any other European country
**Elevation**: north(-west) very flat · centre hilly · far south big mountains (**German Alps** on the Austrian border); plains SE of Regensburg = flat farmland w/ slight ridge NE

**Area codes** grouped by first digit; **postal codes counter-clockwise**: 0 = Saxony → 9 = N-Bavaria/Thuringia; state flags & coats of arms (on unblurred plates); **suffix meta**: -ow/-itz = east (Slavic), -weiler/-ach = south
Guardrails: mostly **B-type**; A-type → northwest + Rhineland-Palatinate + parts of Bavaria

**Bicycle signs**: white w/ green letters; **red letters** → Hamburg/NRW/nearby Lower Saxony; Saarland = white text on dark green; **sign backs** (folded vs flat) + connector types (double/T-shaped) regionguess Germany
**Vegetation**: Baltic pines (orange trunks) → northeast; white-painted tree bottoms → northeast; vineyards → southwest (+few east); hop fields → north of Munich

**Architecture**: brick houses → northwest; thatched roofs + rock walls → North Frisian Islands, Schleswig-Holstein, MV resorts; black **slate 'fish-scale' walls** → central belt; **Commie blocks** → all of former East Germany
**Bollard state metas**
bullets: Darker-grey reflectors → mapped regions
NRW: green (or other-colour) caps; caps w/ **LVR text** = southern NRW only
Bavaria: round wooden bollards w/ painted top; simple orange posts (also other mapped states)
Lower Saxony K-roads (one region): irregular bollards w/o white casing
White bollard plates → mapped states; black **disks under bollards** → east; **orange plates = Rhineland-Palatinate only**; grey plates = Baden-Württemberg only
White posts w/ **red cap** → former East (esp. Saxony-Anhalt); fully **yellow posts** → Brandenburg; yellow + white plate → Saxony-Anhalt
Triangular posts: unpainted rocky + white plates → RLP & Saxony; tall white + plates → Saarland/Thuringia/BW (short in BW; BW also grey); white w/o plates → NRW

hints.text: **Road-marker state metas**: Bavaria = white rectangles split in 3 (left = current road); Lower Saxony = simple rounded rectangles; Schleswig-Holstein + Lower Saxony = metal-bordered markers (LS: smaller post-comma digits); tree markers (tiny green) → Brandenburg & MV

**Pole state metas**: round concrete poles ≈ former East only; trident tops → Bavaria/Saxony-Anhalt/BW; triangle tops (CZ/SK-like) → west; thick-middle-bar tops → former East; curved-bar tops → Bavaria (rare)
Rare white-black **Gen4 EV car** → Hamburg + around Frankfurt (same car: London, Ireland); **Aldi split**: Aldi Süd = south, Aldi Nord = north

**Street-sign city metas**: Berlin = white w/ grey border + number section (note the ß); Munich = blue w/ white decorative border + unique font; Stuttgart = black, borderless; Düsseldorf = black w/ white border + metal frame; Dortmund = blue w/ white border (small towns can imitate!)
**Sorbian** (Slavic) on signs near the Polish/Czech border

**City-state metas**: bus stops — Berlin segmented w/ yellow post; Hamburg red post + hexagon route numbers (also outside HH); Bremen = name in red section; Bremerhaven = grey section variant. Bins — Berlin bright orange; Hamburg red w/ speech bubbles; Bremen green-grey w/ orange bubbles; Bremerhaven generic green. Cologne: **yellow numbers on black plastic** on lampposts
**Saxony**: half-timbered **arch houses** (Umgebindehäuser) in one region; **Sylt**: shrub-and-sand hills unique to the island + Frisian rock walls/thatch/brick
**Coverage spotlights**: low-cam around Singen/Swiss border; Gen3 **black car in the safari park** SE of Bielefeld (zebra-painted 4x4s; Europe-unique); rare Gen3 antenna car in central Hamburg
Gen3 unique car: **roof rack w/ black tape** on the right end + hood **wrap w/ a red rectangle** — usually enough to ID Ghana
Gen4 = grey or white **pickup truck w/ a front antenna** (usually blurred)
Plates **white (private) / yellow (commercial)**, long or short (Nigeria's white plates have a green tint)
Mostly **flat, red soil**; tropical vegetation, palms common toward the south
Taxis have **multicoloured body panels** — fenders & quarter panels always **orange-yellow**
Road lines always **white** (many paved roads have none); **wooden poles w/ 3 insulators** on a metal crossbar (Kenya similar)
Patriotic country — things painted **red/yellow/green (+ black star)**; B&W striped signposts in cities
Coverage limited (densest south: Accra/Kumasi); **3 tape types on the car** (unpeeled SW, peeled E/N, scotched N of Kumasi); north = savannah, SW/E = dense forest + palms
British Overseas Territory at the southern tip of Iberia, bordering Spain — **counts as UK** in country streaks
All coverage = **Gen3** w/ a long **'Gibraltar antenna'** with a silver tip
hints.uniq_note: Found nowhere else in Europe EXCEPT parts of Spain — and those parts don't border Gibraltar.

shared_with.note: The same antenna exists in parts of Spain (not near Gibraltar).

hints.text: The **Rock of Gibraltar**: massive limestone mountain w/ exposed white rock & distinct shape — dominates every part of the territory
Plates **white front / yellow back** (UK-style) — but Gibraltar **drives on the RIGHT** (unlike the UK)
hints.uniq_note: UK plates + right-hand traffic together = Gibraltar.

hints.text: **English** is the main language — English on official signs in a Mediterranean setting

shared_with.note: Malta is the other Mediterranean English-signs place.
shared_with.diff: The Rock + right-hand UK plates → Gibraltar; limestone field walls + triq → Malta.

hints.text: Dense urban, Mediterranean-style pastel buildings w/ **louvered window shutters** (green/blue/brown)
All coverage from **December → Christmas decorations** around town
hints.uniq_note: Christmas decor in a Mediterranean Gen3 round is a strong Gibraltar signal.

hints.text: **Barbary macaques** — the only wild monkeys in Europe
Southern tip: minarets of the **Ibrahim-al-Ibrahim Mosque** — southernmost mosque of continental Europe
Plates all-white or EU blue-strip; **taxis = yellow plates**
**Greek alphabet** — Latin/Cyrillic-like but soft lowercase shapes
**Tall dark-brown wooden poles**; harp-shaped metal poletops (only GR+CY); often **5 vertical insulators**; side lamps w/ round shade (also MK/RS)
Slightly faded **solid white double middle lines** (Mediterranean tell); **yellow lines commoner than anywhere else in southern Europe**
Wedge bollards w/ **almost square reflectors** — red front / white back
Chevrons white-on-black (also AL/IT/UK; Spain = only 2/4 arrows); **warning signs red border + yellow background**
Directional signs blue, arrow-shaped: **Greek in YELLOW + English in white**; town entry = white w/ thick blue stripes top+bottom; km markers blue w/ 'km' in both alphabets
Pedestrian signs: **two dotted horizontal lines** (not stripes)
Roadside **miniature churches on pedestals** — unique to Greece
Mediterranean: white/pastel stone + orange tiles — **white houses commoner than elsewhere**; urban = light multi-floor w/ connected balconies; the famous **white+blue** style mostly on islands
Very mountainous — much winding mountain-road coverage; **red dirt** very common (also Albania)
Smallcam Gen4; much **bleak winter/fall Gen3** coverage
**Island coverage map**: mainland + Crete have all 3 generations, most islands don't; **area codes**: 2 + 1–3 digits (3rd digit 1 = biggest city of the region, 9 = its surroundings)

Big flat farm valleys → Thessaloniki + Larissa; tallest mountains + high-altitude roads → western half; **Cyclades** = white-blue houses + barren bushy islands; olives → coasts (Crete, S-Peloponnese); citrus → S-Peloponnese
**Crete**: unique antenna w/ a slight bulge at the tip
**Mount Pangaion** = solitary mountain south of a flat valley; **Meteora** = monolithic rock towers north of Trikala
**Athens** = yellow taxis; **Thessaloniki** = blue taxis w/ white roof + unusually tall rooftop antennas
**50+ small disjoint coverage patches** (densest: southern west coast) — each with own weather/season; all **Gen3 trekker**, often attached to vehicles
Most locations have their **own odd Street View vehicle** (red tractor, snowmobile w/ box, ATVs…) — the best way to ID the exact town
Small, **colourful simple wooden houses**, sparsely placed even in cities; bigger buildings minimalistic
Very **barren**: no trees or tall vegetation, often snow; mountains/hills, almost everything coastal → **fjords & coastline everywhere**

shared_with.diff: Colourful wooden houses + trekker vehicles → Greenland; black plates → Svalbard.

shared_with.note: Treeless volcanic coasts can look similar.
shared_with.diff: Real roads + Gen4 → Iceland; trekker patches → Greenland.

hints.text: **Boat trekkers very common**, many different boats
**Major towns by vehicle**:
bullets: Nuuk: grey car + by far the largest city
Sisimiut: black snowmobile w/ straight-up mirrors, very overcast/grey
Ilulissat: green ATV w/ black bars, driver in beige hat, sunny
Qaqortoq: red rectangular car, extremely sunny, greener than most
Aasiaat: windshield w/ shiny red-white-black reflection + big round mirrors, sunny, thawing snow
Tasiilaq: ATV w/ large grey handguards, thin clouds, somewhat green
Uummannaq: sharp mountain to the north + green snowmobile w/ big black mirrors
Narsaq: thin antenna right of the car, sunny, fairly green

hints.text: **Smaller settlements**:
bullets: Maniitsoq: tractor coverage, overcast + snowy
Narsarsuaq: driver in black hoodie, sunset sun, unusual bushes
Brattahlíð (across the fjord): black ATV w/ orange-purple bag, tall green grass, reddish dirt
Qeqertarsuaq: blue ATV w/ wide mirrors, overcast, yellow grass
Ittoqqortoormiit: brown wooden box on ATV + purple beanie, stony grey ground, sunny
Igaliku: bright snow-covered range NE + green ATV
Paamiut: barely-visible round grey car, thin clouds, yellow-green grass + red moss
Kangerlussuaq: streaky clouds, dry almost arid sandy look; east = big white truck; road to Sisimiut = thick snow, sunset
Saattut: cloud-shrouded range north across the fjord, muddy paths
Kulusuk: round red ATV, pyramid mountain NE, dark post-sunset
Kangaatsiaq: thawing snow, setting sun, water west
Ikerasak: monolithic mountain west · Ukkusissat: tall cliffside east
Kuummiit: sharp mountain east + round one north, darkish
Tiilerilaaq: GREY houses (unusual!) + smooth light-grey rock ground
Niaqornat: big dark-grey rocks towering · Siorapaluk: sandy pyramid peaks + wide beach ('Pilersuisoq' store)
Qaanaaq: icebergs SW + bright snowy range behind
Kapisillit: red-black ATV, snow-dusted mountains
Kangerluk: evenly tall shaped mountains across the fjord
East of Nuuk: sharp snow-powdered peaks, dark
NE of Narsarsuaq: ice blocks in every direction
Upernavik: covered airport runway = widest paved 'road' in Greenland

hints.text: **Boat coverage**:
bullets: White cabin + red roof: many west-coast towns, mostly around Aasiaat
Red square cabin: west coast, esp. around Akunnaaq
Black box + chequered bow + captain in black coat: NE coast (foggy west, sunny east)
Bright red boat: east coast near Tiilerilaaq (clear) & Kuummiit (overcast)
Black-white boat w/ blue roof: Kuummiit↔Tasiilaq
White boat w/ brown wooden top + instruments: Tiilerilaaq↔Tasiilaq
Small rounded red bow: south of Tasiilaq
Red-black boat w/ several people: far-north east-coast fjord
Wooden fishing boat: outside Ilulissat
White boat w/ silver bow handrail: west of Narsaq (sun west) / east of Qaqortoq (sun east)
Large all-white boat: off Narsarsuaq
Grey boat: east of Nuuk; white boat w/ very tall rear antennas: fjord east of Nuuk

hints.text: US overseas territory (counts as US); car fully blurred but a **front-right antenna + tiny roof-rack** show (the N. Mariana car has NO roof rack)
Poles almost always **thick concrete** (N. Mariana Islands use wooden poles)
**Green** street signs (N. Mariana Islands use blue)
Tropical, dense low vegetation; a very large number of **road reflectors** (also true of N. Mariana)
**East Asian languages** common — Japanese, Chinese, Korean, even Russian
Coverage limited (little inland in the south); **hilly south, flat north**; LeoPalace Resort = white buildings w/ orange tiled roofs
Short plates — blurred they show a **blue tinge or a hint of green on the left**
**Grey Street View car w/ visible roof rack + side mirrors** on ALL car coverage
hints.uniq_note: Only Laos has a similar car among tropical countries; the Dominican Republic's has thick black bar-stripes and no visible mirrors.

shared_with.note: The only other tropical country with a similar car.
shared_with.diff: Spanish + ALTO → Guatemala; Lao script → Laos.

shared_with.note: Similar car but thick black stripes on the bars, mirrors not visible.

hints.text: Tropical country, mainly covered by **rainforest**
**Pastel-painted stone houses w/ rusted sheet-metal roofs**; exposed white brick also common
Roads: solid white outer + single yellow middle — **significantly thinner lines than Mexico's**

shared_with.note: Same scheme in Mexico with thicker lines.
shared_with.diff: Thin lines + grey roof-rack car → Guatemala.

hints.text: Stop signs say **'ALTO'**
Poles very commonly painted **pink, green, or both**
Coverage fairly sparse, especially in the north (map of covered roads)
**Topography**: middle = mountainous; south = completely flat farmland, more built-up; north = fairly flat w/ slight hills/rolling
**Oil-palm plantations** in two areas: southwest + just north of the central mountains

**Completely green poles** → southeast (+ one eastern road further north + a stretch near Melchor de Mencos, far NE); their white **pole plates: top two characters = the road you're on** (infographic)

**Car-bar meta**: top-right bar faces FORWARD in most of the country; in the southeast it faces BACKWARD (often w/ a blue rope on the bottom-right bar); south + northeast also backward but **missing the thin black cap** on the bottom-right bar
Phone numbers: **2 = Guatemala City only**; **6 = Guatemala Department only**; 7 = anywhere EXCEPT Guatemala Dept; 3/4/5 = anywhere

**Spotlights**: Lago de Atitlán = by far the biggest mountain lake, ringed by volcanoes; jungle dirt road north of Tikal; small white bollards w/ 2 black stripes → road 1 NE of Quetzaltenango; PET-11 south of Las Pozas = rolling + perfectly N-S; CA9 (Escuintla→coast) = divided concrete highway
**Tikal trekker**: several Mayan temples just west of Tikal town — the most prominent ruins trekker (others exist, smaller)
White rectangular **'electricista' stickers** on poles = unique to Guatemala City
Most common plate: white w/ an **arcing rainbow** across the centre — outline sometimes visible through the blur; **front plates required**
Only US state w/ its **own language**: Hawaiian place names everywhere — only 8 consonants (H K L M N P W + ʻokina), every word ends in a vowel, macrons **Ā Ē Ī Ō Ū**, many diphthongs (UI/AI/AE…)
Camera mix: lots of Gen4 **smallcam** (low mount, big round blur, car parts visible); Gen3 mostly w/ a **stubby antenna**; fair amount of **Gen2** on rural roads (rare elsewhere in the US)
**Lush tropical vegetation** (ferns, tall grass, flowers) on **volcanic topography**: sloping volcanoes, rugged cliffs, valleys, craters — the combo is the Hawaii tell
hints.uniq_note: Within the US nothing else pairs tropical plants with volcano slopes.

hints.text: Homes: 1–2 storey **wood, light colours**, often w/ **volcanic-rock retaining walls**
Thick **yellow bands** on poles
hints.uniq_note: Don't confuse with California's 3 thin yellow stripes.

hints.text: Highway shields shaped like a **guitar pick** w/ the number at the bottom; mostly on **yellow signposts**
Raised pavement markers in a repeating pattern: **1 reflector – 4 close together – 1**
State-highway **first digit = island**: 1–2 Hawaiʻi (Big Island), 3 Maui, 4 Molokaʻi/Lānaʻi, 5 Kauaʻi, 6–9 Oʻahu; all four 'interstates' are on Oʻahu

**Car = island group**: smallcam w/ **black car** → Hawaiʻi/Maui/Oʻahu; **white car** → Kauaʻi/Molokaʻi/Lānaʻi; Gen2 & non-smallcam Gen4 only on Hawaiʻi/Maui/Oʻahu; a distinct **green blur → only Oʻahu**
**Islands**:
bullets: Kauaʻi: deep red soil + rugged hills w/ jungle-like density (Maui/Molokaʻi have red soil but thinner vegetation)
Oʻahu: steep lush hills everywhere; Honolulu = the only dense tall-building city
Molokaʻi: red soil, dry-looking Gen3, sparse population; Kamakou volcano east = hillier/greener
Lānaʻi: central volcano + abundant Cook pines by Lānaʻi City; coasts = dry grass + shrubs, few buildings
Maui: centre = dry flat grasslands between the two mountain massifs; coasts = lush (bananas, palms)
Hawaiʻi (Big Island): interior = rolling grassy plains + volcanic rock w/ volcano views; coasts = towns + ferns; flat forest roads south of Hilo

hints.text: **Spotlights**: the 'Hawaiian lattice' road grid on Mauna Loa's slope (volcanic soil/rocks, road angles are the clue); Kaunakakai = Molokaʻi's only 'urban' town; **Red Hill** (Maui) = barren red rock ABOVE the clouds; Kahoʻolawe = trekker-only islet SW of Maui (gravel trails, dry shrubs, dark red soil)
Chinese SAR (counts as China in streaks); plates **white front / yellow rear** (some cars carry an extra black mainland plate)

shared_with.note: Also white front / yellow rear plates.
shared_with.diff: Sinhala + tropical island → Sri Lanka; dense skyscrapers → HK.

hints.text: Drives on the **left** (Singapore too; Taiwan drives right)

Ultra-dense city: modern skyscrapers, busy streets — the 'vibe' alone identifies urban HK; **extremely tall apartment towers** (much taller than Singapore/Taiwan); wall-mounted **air conditioners** everywhere
Rural HK: mountainous, green subtropical; low-rise villages but apartment towers rarely far away (Singapore = flat!)
**Bilingual English + traditional Chinese** signs (Cantonese-speaking)

shared_with.diff: Taiwan drives right, much less English.

shared_with.note: Traditional characters + Portuguese instead of English.

hints.text: Cylindrical **grey lamp posts w/ wide base** (+white sticker); rural-only cylindrical wooden poles (single/double, w/ counters/transformers)

Kerbs almost **never striped** (vs Singapore!); grey pedestrian guardrails; **yellow pedestrian crossings** + yellow intersection grids; white centre + white/yellow outer lines (yellow outer = no stopping); concrete roads common
Road signs white rectangular w/ **thick black outline** + bilingual text; give-way = white triangle w/ thick red border + bilingual text
**Weathered rock walls** on mountainsides, some netted, with green/blue plaques
Red **fire hydrants** (circular top, 3 protrusions; pipe variant); yellow bollards w/ **white arrow in blue circle**; bright **orange bins**

**Taxis**: silver roofs + green bumper stickers; red = HK Island/Kowloon, green = New Territories, blue = Lantau/Chek Lap Kok; **double-decker buses** (place names on the front help region-guess; Singapore's are lime-green)
**Rail metas**: double-decker tram along north HK Island (don't confuse w/ red MTR line); NW New Territories = light-rail w/ green-orange platforms, separated tracks

**District looks**: Mong Kok = crowded grid, shops under old apartment blocks (little glass); Central = modern skyscrapers, luxury stores; Mid-Levels = non-identical tall apartments on hills; Quarry Bay = identical white-brown blocks + glass offices; Caribbean Coast/Tung Chung = identical 'wall' apartments + palms; NW border area = flat waterlogged rice paddies + Shenzhen skyline north; boxy village houses → north
**IFC vs ICC**: IFC (HK Island) = rounder + vertical window frames; ICC (Kowloon) = cuboid, no frames — tells you which side you're on

**Bridge metas**: Tsing Ma = only major suspension bridge, ladder towers, yellow-red guardrails; Ting Kau = 3 cylindrical towers + yellow paint; Stonecutters = no yellow, box-beam guardrails; HK-Zhuhai-Macao bridge visible from Chek Lap Kok's South Perimeter Road (b&y barriers)

**Road spotlights**: Lung Wo Road = both skylines visible (Island S, Kowloon N); Tai Chung Kiu Road = NE divided, Shing Mun River west, palm median + bike path (Route 2 = more lanes); Fan Kam Road = narrow, dense tropical, no outer lines, NE; Lam Kam Road = E-W, busier, wider, tree median; Disneyland roads = divided, palms + **teal street lamps**; Chek Lap Kok = large-scale construction + bridges

**Trekker islands**: Peng Chau, Cheung Chau, Lamma (hilliest) = no cars, narrow streets, rural buildings; **Lugard Road** (Victoria Peak) = black guardrail + skyline view + tourists; Kai Tak Cruise Terminal = shiny cylinder w/ triangular windows (seen from Eastern Corridor / Kwun Tong bypass)
Plates long white (2022+ format w/ coat of arms); taxis = yellow, EVs = green
Bollards b&w wedge-shaped, **red front reflector** (blue also common), white back

shared_with.note: Very similar — but reflector nearer the top + thinner.

hints.text: Concrete **holey poles w/ thin holes down to the ground**; wooden poles too

Romanian holes are much wider or taller.

hints.text: Chevrons **white on red** (b&w rarer)
**Hungarian**: many acute accents, ö/ü umlauts, long words — **Ő Ű (double acutes) unique**; also spoken in central Romania + southern Slovakia

hints.text: Street names end **utca** (big roads: **út**) — unique words for 'street'
**Green km markers** along most roads (highway numbers match the yellow exit boxes on the map)
Sign posts **abnormally tall** (often above camera height); signs bolted w/ **4 through-bolts in a tall rectangle** (not common elsewhere in Eastern Europe)

hints.text: Pedestrian sign: 5 stripes + person w/ **belt sagging noticeably LOW** — unique detail (variant: 4 stripes + bag)
Numbered roads in a small **shield** on green signs (highways 'M' on blue); directional signs green (or blue on M) w/ double border
Gen3: **grey flag on the antenna** (also CZ/SK/RO/BG); Gen4 white or **antenna-less black** car; most Gen3 = grey dead **winter** (also CZ/BG)
Light-painted stone houses, almost always **red tiled roofs**; suburbs single-storey; centres older but well-kept
Road lines all-white — but **bicycle lanes use yellow lines**
**Area codes** (2 digits; Budapest = 1); **road-number first digit circles around Budapest**; town-name **prefix map** (local prefixes)
Street sign w/ **thick metal frame** → west; blue/green **arrow street signs on utility poles** → Szabolcs-Szatmár-Bereg (Dunakeszi's are on blue posts)

**SE = pancake-flat**; north+west = forested hills/mountains (tallest north); **Lake Balaton** = the only big lake — hilly, touristy, hotel-dense; **Puszta** (Hortobágy) = barren wet natural grassland (rest of the plains = cultivated)
**Street-sign city metas**: infographic covers the 10 biggest towns w/ unique designs
Road east of **Tornanadaska** (Slovak border): snow on/along the road + bollards + railway right beside
Gen4 = **red & white pickup trucks** (well blurred); Gen3 = white car w/ long antenna

shared_with.diff: Trees → Sweden; treeless volcanic land → Iceland.

hints.text: Plates fully white w/ blue characters, **no EU strip**
**Yellow bollards w/ white reflector** — on almost every rural road
**Barren & treeless**, dark grey volcanic soil — looks like nowhere else in Northern Europe; geothermal lakes, lava fields, rifts, rock towers
Direction signs **yellow** w/ white road-number inset (dashed border = nearby road, not yours; farm signs blue/white)
Pedestrian signs: 4 stripes on a **yellow background** — Europe's only yellow ped signs
**Icelandic: ð/Ð þ æ** + accented vowels (á é í ó ö ú ý)

hints.text: Street signs white/black w/ thin metal rim on lampposts; **all-white road lines** (yellow lines → think Norway/Finland); chevrons black w/ yellow arrows (NO/FI similar); signs w/ yellow background (like SE/FI)
Sparse coverage (densest SW; few interior roads); **road numbers**: ring road = 1; first digit regional, **2 (south) → 9 clockwise**

**Westfjords** = narrow fjords + flat-topped mountains; **east fjords** = larger, more jagged peaks; valleys west of Akureyri face N/NE between snowy peaks; inland = dark volcanic gravel deserts (mostly east); SW = flat farmland w/ distant mountains; Eiðar valley = hilly farms + snowy peaks both sides; Hallormsstaður = NE-SW valley w/ river + **unusually many trees**; Selfoss = lone flat-ridge mountain
**Reykjavík** = the only 'big city' feel, flat on a bay's south side; **Akureyri** = 2nd city in a N-S valley w/ mountains east AND west
**Pickup colour splits the country**: red = southwest, white = northeast

**Spotlights**: Vatnajökull glaciers visible from the southern ring road; only a handful of divided/3+-lane highways (mapped); **Heimaey** = ragged cliffs over Vestmannaeyjabær; very foggy coverage → Bakkagerði (NE)
Most coverage = **"shitcam"** — low quality, brownish colours, often a large circular blur over the car

**Sinhala** round script + lush island vegetation → Sri Lanka.

hints.text: All **Gen4 = smallcam**: mounted lower, big circular blur with a small front protrusion (blur can be fully transparent)
Significant number of **Gen3 trekkers** throughout the country
Drives on the **left**
Wide variety of **scripts** — most narrow down the region (see the state section)
bullets: **English** is co-official and appears on much signage

hints.text: Plates fairly **long**; standard **white**, commercial **yellow**, electric **green**
**Black-and-white striped signposts**

hints.text: Most common pole: **square concrete with a trident poletop**; poles almost always concrete/metal — wooden extremely rare
bullets: Many regions have their own distinct poles/poletops — see the state section

hints.text: **Hindi** (Devanagari: top line through each word, straight verticals) → central & northern regions
**Marathi** — Devanagari like Hindi, but sometimes uses **ळ** (not in Hindi)
**Punjabi** — softer letter shapes, top bar **broken** over some letters; distinct **ਨ ਲ ਅ** + soft-curve diacritic below letters
**Gujarati** — like Hindi but **no top line** over the words
**Bengali** — Devanagari-like but many letters with **left-facing triangles**
bullets: Same script/language as **Bangladesh** — check plates/camera if unsure

hints.text: **Assamese** — Bengali script plus extra characters, notably **ৰ ৱ**
**Kannada** — smooth curves + small circles; often a **squiggly tail** top-right (rarer in Telugu)
**Telugu** — very similar to Kannada, but often **checkmark-like diacritics** on top of letters
**Tamil** — mix of curves, straight lines and **90° angles**; dot-shaped diacritics (also common in Sri Lanka)
**Oriya** — curvy; wide **semi-circles covering letter tops** (mnemonic: looks like the skull emoji 💀)
**Malayalam** — simple curvy letters, many **upside-down U shapes**
**Manipuri (Meitei)** — many vertical lines and right angles; metal trident poles, sometimes small bars on the trident
**Mizo** — Latin script (most common non-English Latin language in Indian coverage); lush green valleys, eroded hillsides with many scattered small peaks (Meghalaya's ridge tops are smoother)
**Almost exclusively English** signage — the only region like this
**Yellow tuk-tuks with black roofs** → south (Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu); rare elsewhere
**Concrete holey poles** — Gujarat + Daman & Diu / Dadra & Nagar Haveli
bullets: Holey poles take precedence over poletop metas
Rarely spill into Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh
hints.uniq_note: Called 'unique to Gujarat (+Daman & Diu, DNH)' by Plonk It — but they can rarely be found in Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh, so confirm with script/soil.

hints.text: Poles **divided in two sections** (Brazil-style)
Metal poles with a **mesh pattern**; taxis often have a **yellow hood** or small yellow circle; Buddhist prayer flags (also Arunachal)
**Metal holey poles**; small **yellow pole stickers** (similar stickers rarely elsewhere); with Goa the most developed states — wealthy modern homes
**Thin grey metal poles** (often two close together) → all of the northeast + Himalayan Uttarakhand & Himachal Pradesh
Electricity counters **mint green** with window-like squares (rarely Haryana); small **window-shaped metal poletops** (Punjab & Haryana); **gurdwaras** (white domes, Khanda symbol) most common here
Trident poletop with **centre insulator directly on the pole top**, outer two on a crossbar below (sometimes Haryana; multi-bar variant exists)
**Upside-down triangle poletops** below the pole top (less common in Madhya Pradesh)
Metal bar shaped like an **upside-down A** slightly below the pole top
**Upside-down trapezoid connector** on poles (also Meghalaya, Tripura, Maharashtra; rare elsewhere) — only use when no other pole meta is present
Poletops attached with two vertical bars forming a **tall narrow rectangle**; variant with two crossbars + diagonal supports to the pole

**Northeast pole metas**: even + uneven poletop combos; '**<**'-shaped double diagonal bars (Meghalaya & Arunachal, occasionally Assam); two crossbars w/ 2 insulators each (Assam/Meghalaya/Mizoram — **>2 bars → Meghalaya or Mizoram**)
Trident poletop with the **middle bar tip bent 90°**
Poletop with a **short crossbar above a longer crossbar**
Pole/tree markings: **one black stripe between two white** stripes
Pole paint: **5+ thin black-and-yellow stripes**; orange & bright-green **buses**
Pole paint: exactly **4 thicker black-and-yellow stripes**; trees with **4 red-white stripes** (also Jharkhand, less often)

**Roof metas**: central = rounded, disorganised tiles; south = organised flat bright-orange tiles w/ middle ridge (esp. Tamil Nadu; darker brown further north); West Bengal = flat tiles w/ **diamond protrusion** + large corrugated **hip roofs** (also nearby Bihar/Odisha); northeast = metal roofs w/ small top triangle + houses of square panels
**Thatched huts** → east coast + northern plains (big hay mounds too)
bullets: UP → Odisha: thatch of **fine hay**; Odisha → Tamil Nadu: **dried palm fans**

hints.text: **Pine metas**: chir pines (smooth conical crowns, long needles) → Uttarakhand/Himachal/J&K/W-Arunachal; khasi pines (jagged, disorganised crowns) → Meghalaya & Manipur
**Cacti fences** (Euphorbia) → Gujarat, W-Maharashtra, S-Rajasthan

**Palm metas**: palmyra (lollipop shape) in numbers → south/east; areca (very thin trunk, small crown) → SW coast, northeast, Andaman & Nicobar; date palms → dry central regions; oil-palm plantations → Andhra panhandle (near Telangana border)
**Crop metas**: tea → northeast + far south (flat tea = Assam / NE-West-Bengal); sugarcane → around Uttar Pradesh + southwest; cotton → Gujarat & Maharashtra; canola → around Rajasthan
**Himalaya** = by far the tallest mountains; **Ladakh/N-Himachal** = extremely dry high-altitude desert; **northern plains** (Ganges valley) = fertile, extremely flat open fields
**Soil metas**: distinctly dark soil → Maharashtra + adjacent; red soil → SW coast + around Chhattisgarh/W-Odisha; sandy dry → W-Rajasthan
**Western Ghats**: tabletop ridgelines (plateaus, not peaks); N drier, S greener — rainy season turns everything green
**Boxy square cars** visible in mapped areas (Odisha variant shows a hood); northeast: fences of thin wooden sticks in a square pattern
Black **pole stickers with digits + a middle arrow**
Planned city: blue **sector signs**, round yellow marks on street lights, street signs with **numbers instead of names** (nearby SAS Nagar & Panchkula similar)
Separated **middle bus lanes** on large roads (people still drive in them)
**Black taxis with yellow roofs**
**Elevated railways** → Bengaluru, Chennai, Delhi, Hyderabad, Jaipur, Kochi, Kolkata, Mumbai
Roofs of **thin grey bricks**; same bricks/rocks in walls & stockyards
NW-Bihar/NE-UP: **white-painted clay huts**; central Bihar: wooden huts with a **cross pattern**

**Tree spotlights**: Japanese cedar (tall, narrow fluffy crowns) → Sikkim/WB border + Meghalaya; Himalayan cedar → NH34 Harsil–Gangotri + Shimla; Bhutan white pine (hanging needles) → Mechuka valley + Bhutan border (Arunachal)
**Doum palms** (palmyra-like but multi-branched)
hints.uniq_note: 'Almost exclusive' to Diu — a stray doum palm elsewhere is possible, so confirm with the coastal Portuguese-influenced townscape.

hints.text: Endangered **Indian pitcher plant** — exclusive to the Khasi Hills (roadside esp. NH206 north of Umlārem)
Western half far more **barren** (logging/erosion); NW of Dehradun: barren brown-grass hills, small cliff-side roads; **Chaukhamba** massif near Ukhimath: snow, three peaks, centre tallest
The **only NE-India spot with visible snowy mountains**; brownish grass + Bhutan white pines; north of Mechuka increasingly overcast/dark

South of Shillong: **V-shaped green eroded valleys** with flat smooth ridgetops (Mizoram = less smooth, more rises/falls); NH6/NH206 SE of Shillong: khasi pines + **yellow outer road lines** + fresh dark pavement (only Meghalaya road with yellow outer lines)
**Salt flats & coastal marshes**, near-zero vegetation, shallow water pools (W-Rajasthan is sandier with desert shrubs)
Southern tip: huge **monolithic rock mountains** (Magenthiragiri hills visible from afar); flat bushy landscape with **very red soil** in the south
Abundant **coconut trees**, often painted **red-and-white** (like the Polish flag)
**NH326** west of Brahmapur: thin grey antenna + unusually high-quality shitcam (best near Harabhangi Reservoir)

videos.title: How You Know You're in Indonesia!
How to recognize Indonesia in GeoGuessr

hints.text: Plates **black with white text** — usually **three white sections** through the blur (commercial = yellow; newer coverage can be white)

shared_with.note: Malaysian plates are black too — but generally only **two** white sections.
shared_with.diff: Count the sections: 3 → Indonesia, 2 → Malaysia. Language spelling helps too (televisi vs televisyen).

hints.text: Drives on the **left**

shared_with.diff: Plate sections + all-white Malaysian road lines.

shared_with.note: The Philippines drives on the **right** — an instant separator.

hints.text: Centre lines **white or yellow** (solid or dashed); outer lines always white — yellow centre is rare in Gen3

shared_with.diff: Any yellow centre line → Indonesia, not Malaysia.

hints.text: Poles **round**, steel or concrete; steel often painted **black**, many with **Indonesian flag colours** (red/white); pole tops even or uneven by region
**Black-and-white** square/circular bollards + **yellow-and-black** ones (sometimes red reflector)
bullets: UK-style bollards mostly in **Kalimantan**

hints.text: **Yellow waystones angled towards the road** with a cutout near the top (3 town shorthands, biggest on top)
hints.uniq_note: Plonk It calls these 'unique to Indonesia'. The Philippines has waystones too — but **perpendicular** to the road with text facing away, so the angled orientation is the tell.

hints.text: **Indonesian** looks like Malaysian, but was influenced by **Dutch** (Malaysian by English): televisi (≈ Dutch televisie) vs Malaysian televisyen

shared_with.diff: Dutch-style spellings (-si, -tas) → Indonesia; English-style (-syen, -titi) → Malaysia. Plates: 3 vs 2 white sections.

hints.text: Rows of **red-and-white banners** everywhere (sometimes more colourful flags)
**Cigarette ads** everywhere — black/red schemes, ~18,000 Rp, graphic pictures, the word **PERINGATAN**
Motorcycles have **front licence plates** (unlike the rest of SE Asia)
hints.uniq_note: Within covered Southeast Asia, front plates on motorcycles are an Indonesia tell — other SEA countries' bikes don't carry them.

hints.text: Currency = **Rupiah (Rp)** on price signs
**Coverage map**: no Papua coverage; sparse in Kalimantan, Aceh, Central Sulawesi, North Maluku
**Kabupaten** (regency, 'Kab.') names on signs/billboards everywhere — 417 covered ones, learnable
Cardinal directions in addresses: Utara=N, Timur=E, Selatan=S, Barat=W, Tengah=Central, Tenggara=SE
**Satellite dishes** point toward mid-West-Kalimantan → tells you N/S of the equator + rough side of the country (angle ≈ distance)
**Postal codes** (5 digits) group by first digit: 1xxxx Jakarta → 9xxxx Maluku/Papua
Pole tops **even vs uneven** (symmetry of the insulator bar) — the base region-guessing meta
Poles with an **extra-long support** on the pole top (sometimes two); drier, low shrubby vegetation (Java can look similar — double-check)
Poles with a **triangle attachment** under the horizontal bar → mapped regions (not valid on stacked poles)
Poles with an **indent on both sides**
**Trident pole tops** (rarely S/W-Kalimantan, N-Sumatra, Riau)
Pole tops with an **extra attachment right under the horizontal bar**
Even bar with **uneven insulators** (1 vs 2) — almost exclusive to North Sumatra
**A-frame pole supports** (also Nusas) — caution: A-frames appear anywhere wires bend; green paint on bridge sides → S/Central Kalimantan

Crossbar with the **flat part facing away** from the pole (normally it faces the pole); painted **white+blue/red fences**; Central Sulawesi: unique **black-white-yellow bollards** + thin black secondary poles with light-blue/green tape (East Peninsula)
**Rectangle attachment** on pole tops (mostly Bali, with the Nusa support beam); pole paint with **horizontal text** (usually dark blue, also light blue/green)
Asymmetrical poles with **2 supports below the bar** (rare in Kalimantan/Java)
**Oil-palm plantations** → Sumatra & Kalimantan (rare in Sulawesi); **rice** → Java (some S-Sulawesi & S-Sumatra)
Very **rounded green hills** with palm/sugarcane/corn clusters
**Savannah** landscape on north Sumba (Gen3 there = always white car); distinct **high-pitched roof peaks**
**Red soil with white topsoil** spilling onto the road

**Roof metas**: tiled roofs → Java (+S-Sumatra, Nusas, S-Kalimantan — there flatter/less rounded); plain sheet metal → north (Kalimantan, Sulawesi, Sumatra); two-level symmetric triangle metal roofs → mainly Sulawesi

**Bugis roofs**: horizontal slots inside the roof triangles (rarely E-Kalimantan/W-Nusa); **gable horns** common here too (also Riau, C/S-Kalimantan); around **Rante Pao**: unique elevated **Tongkonan** saddle-roof houses
**Hindu-Balinese architecture**: crown-like roof pinnacles everywhere, guardian statues, slit-drum towers, dark volcanic **andesite** gates/walls/carvings
Distinct **boat-shaped roofs** (Minangkabau); North Sumatra's version is less angled, without spires — most common near **Lake Toba**
Roofs made of **wooden chips**
Muslim-majority country — **churches/Christian signs** are a strong clue for the mapped (Christian) regions
**Gen3 antenna** coverage → mostly Java (esp. East Java; very rarely Denpasar/Bali); **black Gen3 car** → almost everywhere, most common in Sulawesi
**Moiré-pattern camera glitch** throughout Central Kalimantan
Mountains around the lake + **Samosir Island** in the middle; overcast coverage typical
**Karst formations** in the east, esp. along the highway to East Kalimantan (similar karst: S-Sulawesi, W-Sumatra)
**Palu Bay**: NW-facing bay north of Palu — mountains west, flat urban east
Steeply pitched **palm-leaf roofs** (SW of North Sumatra)
Rare cars: **white SUV with black bars + wooden roof plank** (east Bali); **black cab with men in the truck bed** near Jagasatru
All coverage = a **single trekker inside the Iraqi National Museum, Baghdad**
Camera is **'Gen 1.5'** — Gen2-like but worse quality, often blindingly overexposed; trekker blur has a well-defined **pointed** shape
The only outdoor part = a small **patio w/ a diagonal walkway + date palms**
Interior = **statues, steles, artefacts** and a few back rooms
Plates long **white front AND back** + blue strip left

shared_with.note: UK = white front but **yellow back**, mostly without the strip.

hints.text: **Yellow dashed outer road lines** — very common (essentially never in the UK)

shared_with.diff: NZ = Pacific vegetation + different signage; Ireland = hedgerows + Irish bilingual signs.

hints.text: **Only European country with yellow diamond warning signs** (regulatory signs stay red-border/white)
Signage **bilingual English + Irish (italics)** — places, words and phrases translated
Chevrons **yellow-on-black** (UK: white); yield sign says **YIELD** (UK: GIVE WAY)
Pole warning sticker: **lightning bolt but NO man being zapped** + often a second outlined rectangular sticker (UK sticker shows the man)
**Green-white bollards** (not fully unique but great in UK 50-50s); rural gates w/ **rounded corners** (UK: square — but N. Ireland also rounded!)
**Boreens** — tiny rural roads w/ a grass strip down the middle (strong in UK 50-50s); drives **left**; smallcam Gen4
**Cream-white buildings w/ black roofs**, sometimes grey corner stones; row buildings in cities (British brick still occurs)

Very green agricultural land + **hedgerows** — often indistinguishable from the UK; wooden poles w/ **pole steps** + occasional crossbars (also UK/SE/FI); **double yellow no-parking lines** (also UK/Malta); B&W poles w/ yellow bulb at crossings (like UK)

shared_with.diff: Decide via: chevron colour, YIELD, plate backs, pole stickers, gate corners, diamond signs.

hints.text: Regional roads 3-digit: **first digit = region, anti-clockwise from Dublin** (8/9 = newer, non-regional; ~half of the 8s = Dublin metro); **area codes grouped by first digit**

**West coast**: rugged hills, wild brown-yellow moorland, exposed rock (vs neat green east); **Donegal**: tiny hilly rural roads w/ Gen4; **Wicklow Mountains** (S of Dublin) look western — but never a coast view + Gen4 small roads
**EV Gen4 car** (grey back w/ black semicircle) → most of the east + parts of the NW (Dublin also has the normal blue car)
**Tabletop mountains** → N-Sligo/Leitrim; **Burren NP** = grey limestone karst; **Cork** = the hilly big city
British Crown dependency **between the UK and Ireland** — counts as UK in streaks
Coverage is **Gen2 only**: lower quality, circular blur above & below, slightly off colours
hints.uniq_note: An all-Gen2 British-looking island round is IoM or Jersey — IoM is gloomier/hillier, Jersey sunnier with French names.

hints.text: Plates UK-style (white front / yellow rear) but w/ a **red strip on the left**
hints.uniq_note: The red strip separates IoM from mainland UK & Jersey plates.

shared_with.note: Same white/yellow scheme, no red strip there.

hints.text: Landscape: **hilly farmland** w/ narrow roads through it
Houses w/ **whitewashed walls + entrance walls**

shared_with.diff: Gen2-only + red-strip plates → IoM; Gen3/4 + green Eircode plates → Ireland.

hints.text: **Striped kerbs** sometimes along main roads
**Blue bollards** common in urban areas
**Gorse** bushes (bright yellow flowers) very common

shared_with.note: Gorse also in rugged UK areas (e.g. Scottish Highlands).

hints.text: Generally hilly; the **north is very flat** w/ mountains visible to the south; **Laxey** sits in a NW-SE valley; a **'twig smudge'** on the left lens → southern IoM
**Lamp-post sticker meta** (region decider):
bullets: Generic lamp w/ blue base + small blue sticker = most of the island; top 2 letters → 1 of the 21 parishes
All-green lamps → Ramsey & Castletown (rarer: Malew, Santon, Garff)
White sticker w/ 2 green numbers → Douglas
Yellow sticker → southern Onchan; its 2 letters = road-name initials ('SD' = Sunningdale Drive)

hints.text: **Douglas** (capital): rows of **terraced houses**, higher density; street signs w/ the **oval coat of arms** → Ramsey; arms ABOVE the text → Port Erin
**Spotlights**: Ronaldsway Airport = large open field w/ boundary fence; **Peel Castle** on its island → Peel
Israeli plates **yellow with blue strip left** (also common in the West Bank)
hints.uniq_note: Yellow+blue-strip plates are specific to Israel — Jordan and the UAE use white plates, Tunisia black; but the design also dominates in the West Bank (same page here).

hints.text: Most Gen3: **black car with long antenna** (also black w/o antenna, silver w/ short antenna)
**Hebrew** (practically only here) + **Arabic** (esp. West Bank)
Landscape: greener **Mediterranean north** → very **arid south**
Bright, warm **stone buildings**, blocky; orange tiled roofs commoner than elsewhere in the Middle East, flat roofs equally common
Most roads: **yellow outer + white centre lines**; striped kerbs everywhere (red-white, black-white; rarely red-yellow, blue-white)

shared_with.note: Yellow outer lines also in UAE/Jordan/Oman.
shared_with.diff: White plates + desert + Gulf architecture → UAE; Hebrew → Israel.

hints.text: Both **A-type and B-type guardrails** common

shared_with.note: The only other Middle-Eastern country where B-type guardrails are common.

hints.text: Chevrons **white on black**; km markers white with thick **red/green/blue border**; stop sign shows a **hand** instead of a word
hints.uniq_note: The hand stop sign is a strong tell — but check for Hebrew to be safe (a few other countries use symbol stop signs on private roads).

hints.text: Poles: **black-white striped painted bottoms** + very common **mesh-framework poles**; yellow warning sign on poles
**Coverage**: sparse in southern Israel + most of the West Bank; **area codes** map (only mapped codes are regional)
**Gen4 only in Israel** (black car); mostly a **dirty front camera** — clean camera ≈ Tel Aviv area

**North** = mountainous, green, forested; **centre** = flat, urban, better highways; **south + eastern West Bank** = extremely dry/sandy; **west of Jerusalem** looks like the north — but always hilly (no wide flat valleys) + Jerusalem-stone houses; **West Bank** = semi-arid Mediterranean, hillier & less agricultural than central Israel
**Jerusalem stone**: nearly every building in Jerusalem + nearby West Bank cities (smoother on new buildings; single such houses exist elsewhere)
**Date-palm plantations** near the Jordanian border; cemented **rock walls** in the north; small **irrigation canals** around Beit She'an

**Water bodies**: Dead Sea = surrounded by total dryness (also visible from Jordan/West Bank); Sea of Galilee = noticeably vegetated; **Mount Carmel** = long NW-SE ridge near Haifa (green, high-altitude coverage on top); **Mount Gilboa** = very dry from afar, green on top
**West Bank plates**: white w/ green text (blurred = green tint); taxis/buses inverted (green w/ white); Israeli plates also common there
**West Bank follow cars**: small white Suzuki → centre/south; dark-blue Ford → northwest; big white Citroën 'SECURITY' → around Jerusalem (Bethlehem, Ramallah; always with antenna-less black car)

**Road/mountain spotlights**: Road 90 (longest) = dry + N-S angle (≈ roads 31/40!), southern stretch has a dry range east + palms; Road 2 = Mediterranean visible west (mountains east → Haifa↔Zikhron Yaakov); Mount Tavor = soft round shape; Timna NP = total dryness + ridges all around; tall rocky mountains east of **Eilat**; camel-back range → Kiryat Shmona; reservoir north of Hasolelim forest; tall coastal chimneys north of Netanya
Roadsides **full of parked cars** (free spots → probably the north of the city)
White rectangular signs with a **thin blue border**

**Jericho**: date palms in sparse, poor-looking urban setting; **Ramallah**: sheer mass of black water tanks on most houses; **Bethlehem**: blue house numbers w/ Arabic + Eastern-Arabic numerals split by a diagonal

**City-unique infra**: Harish grey mosaic tower brickwork; Nahariya 3-arm white lamps; Acre 'Barad-dûr' lamps (central-Israel lookalikes lack the horn gap); Netivot angular high+low lamps; Eilat blue-white pole bottoms; Kiryat Motzkin yellow fences/posts; Ashdod blue-white fences; Raanana thick/cube roundabout signs; Rahat grey Hyundai follow car
**House-number metas**: Rosh Haayin = blue w/ **G-clef**; Ness Ziona = Israeli flag; Herzliya = blue shield shape; **Bnei Brak** = ultra-Orthodox religious clothing on the street
**Israel National Trail**: one of the world's longest trekkers, through every landscape; orange-blue-white **trail markings**
Plates: **blue strips both sides + distinctly short front plate** — the combination is unique
hints.uniq_note: Albania also has two blue strips and France a small right strip — but neither has the short front plate; short front + two strips = Italy.

hints.text: Bollards **triangular, white w/ black top**, red front / white rear reflector

shared_with.diff: Albanian language + Gen4 quality + poorer roads → Albania.

hints.text: Very common: **solid white outer lines with NO middle line** (rarer in other Mediterranean countries)
**Italian**: double consonants, many i's, ì é à, words end in vowels; street = **via**; 'Passo carrabile' no-parking signs w/ comune name on top
Mediterranean: farmland on rolling hills + forested mountain valleys
Pastel stone houses w/ **terracotta roofs**; wooden **two-part sideways-opening shutters**
Many **round concrete poles** (unusual for the Mediterranean); wooden poles w/ small white sticker; **concrete trident poletops**
**Double guardrails** very common; guardrails w/ **red front reflectors** (Spain: yellow); distinct endings — flatter, wider, bending away
**Black/dark-grey sign backs** common

shared_with.diff: Romanian ă î ș ț → Romania.

hints.text: Directional signs blue w/ white arrow, **ALL-CAPS**; Strada Statale = blue w/ white-blue border; Autostrade = green; km markers = white squares w/ **roman numeral (100 m) over arabic (km)**; road numbers SS/SR/SP (State/Regional/Provincial)
